The slow, rounded little pine of the Colorado Plateau, bearing the rich edible pine nuts central to Southwestern cuisine and Indigenous foodways. Supremely drought- and cold-hardy.
The giant columnar emblem of the Sonoran Desert, growing for over a century and crowning itself with white flowers (Arizona's state flower) and red fruit. A high-rise habitat for desert birds.
A stout barrel ribbed with hooked red spines, leaning south with age and ringed by orange-to-yellow flowers in late summer. Pineapple-scented fruit follows for wildlife.
The classic broad-padded prickly pear, with big yellow blooms and magenta fruit (tunas) eaten fresh or made into jelly. Forms sprawling thickets that shelter desert wildlife.
A deceptively fuzzy-looking cholla densely armored in straw-colored barbed spines that detach at a touch. Its dropped joints root to form spiny colonies across the open desert.
A clustering hedgehog cactus that opens large, vivid magenta cups among its spiny stems in spring, followed by edible strawberry-like fruit. A jewel of rocky desert slopes.
A tidy, ice-blue rosette of broad spine-tipped leaves that, after decades, sends up a towering candelabra of yellow flowers and then dies, leaving offsets. Supremely cold-hardy for an agave.
A tree-form yucca with a slender trunk topped by a fountain of grassy blades and tall spikes of creamy bells. Pollinated by the yucca moth and an icon of the Chihuahuan grasslands.
A striking silver-blue sphere of saw-toothed leaves (sotol) that throws up a tall, plumed flower stalk. Architecturally bold and tough, and the traditional source of the spirit sotol.
Not a true yucca but a grassy evergreen clump sending up arching coral-red flower wands for months that hummingbirds work tirelessly. One of the toughest, longest-blooming desert accents.
A fan of long, thorny, whip-like canes that leaf out within days of rain and tip themselves with flame-red flower clusters for hummingbirds. A living fence and a Sonoran signature.

The most drought-enduring shrub in North America and the defining plant of the warm deserts, scenting the air with 'rain' after a storm. Resinous evergreen leaves, yellow flowers, and clones that live thousands of years.
A silvery, mounding desert shrub that floats yellow daisies on long stalks above its felted leaves each spring. A reliable, fast bloomer that colors roadsides across the Southwest.
A leathery gray-green evergreen whose seeds yield the famous liquid wax 'jojoba oil.' Tough, long-lived, and an excellent waterwise hedge or screen.
A low, airy desert legume whose flowers are puffs of pink-red stamens like tiny powderpuffs, beloved by hummingbirds and bees. A nitrogen-fixer that blooms after winter rains.
A sprawling, nearly leafless desert shrub hung with tubular red flowers through the cool season, a winter lifeline for hummingbirds. Its name, chuparosa, means 'hummingbird' in Spanish.
A silvery evergreen (cenizo) that bursts into clouds of orchid-purple flowers a day or two after summer rains, earning it the name 'barometer bush.' Supremely heat- and drought-proof.
A silvery-stemmed western shrub that blazes gold in late summer when little else blooms, swarming with migrating butterflies and bees. A tough pioneer of dry, disturbed, alkaline ground.
A compact, nearly everblooming shrubby sage in jewel tones of red, coral, and pink that hummingbirds chase from spring to frost. Tidy, tough, and a mainstay of waterwise gardens.
Woolly silver foliage topped by bright yellow daisies on long stalks for most of the year, reseeding into cheerful drifts. One of the longest-blooming and easiest desert wildflowers.
An airy desert shrub bearing single white roses followed by feathery pink seed plumes that glow in the light. It blooms and fruits at once and stabilizes dry, rocky slopes.
A low, aromatic evergreen mound of needle-fine leaves that covers itself in golden daisies for much of the year. Resinous and deer-proof, perfect for hot rock gardens.
A grassy-looking evergreen forming a fountain of long, wiry, flexible leaves topped by a plume of tiny cream flowers. A soft, fine-textured anchor for dry, rocky desert gardens.
Tall wands of glowing rose-pink bells light up the Sonoran spring and draw early hummingbirds. A fast, free-seeding penstemon for desert gardens.
Brilliant scarlet tubular flowers march up tall stems in spring, built for hummingbird pollination. A tough, reliable penstemon for hot, dry, rocky ground across the Southwest.
A silvery desert mallow studded with apricot-orange cups through much of the year, a favorite of specialist desert bees. It thrives on heat, drought, and poor soil.
A neat, long-lived mound that covers itself for months in honey-scented white daisies with golden centers. One of the toughest and tidiest natives for hot, dry, rocky gardens.
Yellow daisies with maroon-backed rays that genuinely smell of chocolate, strongest in the cool morning air. A charming, long-blooming, drought-tough prairie-and-desert wildflower.
A sprawling mound that opens enormous, fragrant white trumpets at dusk for night-flying hawkmoths. Dramatic and easy, but every part is highly poisonous.
A low, spreading desert verbena smothered in rounded clusters of lavender flowers nearly year-round after rains. A tireless nectar source and quick groundcover for dry ground.
A fountain-shaped bunchgrass topped by airy, pink-tinged seed clouds, thriving in salty, alkaline, and seasonally wet desert flats where few grasses persist.
A stately blue-green bunchgrass sending up tall silvery-purple flower spikes in fall. A bold, elegant accent grass that shrugs off heat, drought, and clay.
A delicate bunchgrass whose long, reddish-purple awns catch the light like a shimmering haze, especially backlit. A pioneer that greens up and flowers after rain on poor, dry soils.
The towering backbone of Pacific Northwest forests and the region's most important timber tree, with soft needles and three-pronged bracts peeking from its cones. Fast-growing and long-lived.
A graceful, shade-loving conifer with a nodding leader and lacy, feathery foliage, dominant in the wet old-growth. Washington's state tree and a quiet giant of the understory.
The 'tree of life' to Northwest Coast peoples, with fragrant fanned foliage and fibrous cinnamon bark used for canoes, planks, and baskets. Thrives in wet ground and deep shade.
A massive, salt-tolerant spruce of the fog-soaked coast, with stiff, sharply pointed needles. The largest spruce in the world and a defining tree of the coastal temperate rainforest.
A sprawling, multi-stemmed understory maple with delicate red-and-white spring flowers and brilliant scarlet fall color. Sculptural and graceful in woodland shade.
The West's flowering dogwood, opening large creamy bracts in spring (often again in fall) and ripening red fruit for birds. An exquisite but disease-sensitive woodland tree.
The Northwest's iconic prairie-savanna oak, a slow, gnarled tree supporting an extraordinary web of wildlife. The keystone of the region's vanishing oak woodlands.
The stately pine of the dry interior West, with long needles, cinnamon-puzzle bark that smells of butterscotch in the sun, and fire-adapted open groves. A drought-tough giant.
A modest understory tree with glossy ribbed leaves and dark berries that birds devour, historically harvested for its medicinal bark. Adaptable in sun or deep shade.
The dominant evergreen groundcover of Northwest forests, with leathery leaves, urn-shaped pink-white flowers, and edible blue-black berries. Tough in dry shade and prized in floral greenery.
Oregon's state flower, a glossy holly-leaved evergreen with fragrant golden flower clusters and tart blue 'grapes' for birds and jelly. Adaptable from sun to shade.
A low, fern-like evergreen Oregon grape that carpets the forest floor with long glossy leaflets, yellow spring flowers, and blue berries. Ideal for dry woodland shade.
A refined evergreen huckleberry with glossy bronze new growth, pink bell flowers, and sweet black late-season berries. Slow but elegant, taking sun on the coast and shade inland.
An early-blooming thicket shrub whose magenta flowers feed returning hummingbirds and whose orange-to-red berries ripen first of the natives. Vigorous in moist, shady ground.
A thornless, big-maple-leaved shrub with crinkled white roses and soft, sweet-tart red berries shaped like thimbles. Spreads to fill moist woodland edges and clearings.
Arching branches drip with foamy, cream-colored flower plumes in early summer that fade to tan and persist. A drought-tough pollinator shrub whose hard wood was prized for tools.
Clouds of intensely orange-blossom-scented white flowers smother this shrub in early summer. Idaho's state flower and a drought-tolerant beauty for sunny banks.
A fast, arching streamside shrub with rounded white flower clusters, reddish seed heads, and peeling, layered bark. Excellent for stabilizing wet banks and rain gardens.
A tough, suckering shrub with tiny pink bell flowers followed by clusters of waxy white berries that persist into winter. Thrives in sun or shade, wet or dry, holding difficult banks.
The first native shrub to leaf and bloom each year, hanging fragrant greenish-white bells in late winter for early pollinators, then bluish plum-like fruit for birds. A cheerful harbinger of spring.
A multi-season shrub with starry white spring flowers, sweet blue-purple summer berries (saskatoons) for pies and birds, and fiery fall color. Adaptable and beloved across the West.
Washington's state flower, a stately wild rhododendron lighting the conifer understory with big trusses of rose-pink bells in late spring. Wants acidic, well-drained woodland soil.
A hardy wild rose with large, fragrant single pink blooms and big red hips that feed birds and make tea. Suckers into thickets that shelter wildlife along forest edges and wetlands.
The blue tide of Northwest wet meadows in spring, its starry flowers rising over grassy leaves. Its bulbs were a staple food cultivated by Indigenous peoples for millennia.
The taller, statelier camas, with elegant spires of blue-violet or creamy stars in late spring. Superb in moist meadows and a magnet for early bees.
The beloved 'wake-robin' of Northwest woods, opening a single three-petaled white flower above a whorl of three leaves that blushes pink with age. Slow to establish, and best left undisturbed.
A dainty meadow iris with tough, grassy leaves and lavender-purple flowers veined in gold. One of the easiest Pacific Coast irises, drought-tolerant once established.
Ferny blue-green foliage hung with nodding pink heart-shaped flowers for months in cool shade. Spreads gently to form a soft woodland groundcover.
Scalloped, semi-evergreen leaves send up slender wands of small fringed cups that age from white to pink. An easy, shade-loving woodland filler that reseeds politely.
Glossy, heart-shaped evergreen leaves form a lush mat in deep shade, hiding curious long-tailed maroon flowers at ground level. The crushed foliage smells of ginger.
Bold spires of blue-purple pea flowers tower over handsome palmate leaves in early summer. A nitrogen-fixing meadow lupine and the ancestor of the famous Russell hybrids.
Woolly silver foliage smothered in bright golden daisies through early summer, thriving on hot, dry, rocky ground. A cheerful, drought-proof pollinator plant for sunny banks.
Clustered heads of deep blue-purple bells top leafy stems in summer, tolerating more moisture and shade than most penstemons. An easy, bee-loved native for westside gardens.
Nodding, swept-back white flowers with golden centers rise over mottled leaves in early spring, then vanish by summer. A delicate woodland-and-meadow ephemeral worth the wait.
The signature evergreen fern of the Northwest forest floor, with bold, leathery, sword-shaped fronds in big shuttlecock clumps. Tough, long-lived, and the workhorse of shade gardens.
A tidy evergreen fern with two kinds of fronds: a flat rosette of sterile blades and stiffly upright fertile ones. Elegant in moist, acidic shade and important winter browse for deer and elk.
Delicate, fan-shaped green leaflets shimmer on wiry black stems near seeps and waterfalls. A graceful, airy fern for consistently moist, shaded spots.
A cool-season meadow bunchgrass forming dense tufts topped by airy, shimmering flower clouds that catch the light. Thrives in moist, even wet, sun or part shade.
A robust, evergreen wetland sedge with arching dark-green blades and drooping flower spikes. A backbone plant for rain gardens, pond edges, and bioswales in the Northwest.
The most widespread tree in North America, famed for white bark and fluttering leaves that turn the mountainsides gold in fall. A single clonal grove can be among the largest organisms on Earth.
A slim, straight-trunked pine forming dense fire-adapted forests across the high country, its cones often sealed with resin until heat opens them. A pioneer after burns.
A gnarled, wind-sculpted pine of exposed ridges with branches so pliable they can be tied in a knot. Its large seeds feed nutcrackers and bears at treeline.
Colorado's state tree, a stiff, conical spruce prized for powder-blue, sharply pointed needles. Native to cool mountain streamsides but planted far beyond its range.
A stately fir with soft, blue-green, citrus-scented needles and a symmetrical spire. The most heat- and drought-tolerant of the true firs and a favorite for its color and form.
The West's answer to the sugar maple, lighting canyon slopes with flame-red and orange fall color. A tough, drought-tolerant small tree for the interior mountains.
A narrow, blue-green-foliaged juniper of dry foothills and bluffs, with frosted berry-like cones that feed waxwings and robins in winter. Extremely drought- and cold-hardy.
A slow, dense, evergreen small tree of dry rocky slopes, with leathery curled leaves and feathery corkscrew seed tails. A nitrogen-fixer and vital winter browse, with ironhard wood.
A sagebrush-country shrub covered in small fragrant yellow flowers in spring, then nutritious seeds. A nitrogen-fixer and one of the most important browse plants for deer and antelope.
The defining shrub of the Intermountain West, perfuming millions of acres with its silvery, three-toothed evergreen leaves. Essential habitat for sage-grouse, pygmy rabbits, and pronghorn.
A low, green-stemmed shrub that erupts in golden flower clusters in late summer, feeding migrating butterflies and bees when the steppe is otherwise spent. Tough and drought-proof.
A compact, aromatic currant of dry mountain slopes with tubular pinkish-white flowers for hummingbirds and translucent red berries for birds. Thrives on hot, rocky exposures.
A rock-loving mountain shrub with clusters of waxy, fragrant white-to-pink flowers and rich russet fall color. Clings to cliffs and crevices in the Rockies.
A tough foothill shrub with small wedge-shaped leaves and feathery, spiraling seed plumes that shimmer in autumn. A nitrogen-fixer and important browse for dry, rocky slopes.
A glossy, aromatic evergreen ceanothus that foams with white flower clusters after mountain snowmelt. A nitrogen-fixing fire-follower that bees and butterflies swarm.
The common wild rose of the interior West, with fragrant pink blooms and bright hips that feed birds and make tea. A hardy, suckering thicket-former for hedgerows and stream banks.
A graceful mountain shrub with pink tubular flowers for hummingbirds and waxy white winter berries for birds. Adaptable and suckering, good for naturalizing dry slopes.
A hardy, silver-flecked shrub bearing tart red berries that are a key food for bears and birds. A nitrogen-fixer for cold, dry, rocky mountain soils.
A low, spreading evergreen groundcover with holly-like leaves that bronze in winter, golden spring flowers, and blue berries. Tough in dry shade across the mountain West.
A compact, mounding shrub of rocky mountain slopes with rounded white-pink flower clusters, reddish seedheads, and peeling bark. Reliable fall color and easy in lean soil.
Colorado's state flower, lifting large, long-spurred blue-and-white blooms over ferny foliage in mountain meadows and aspen groves. Pollinated by hawkmoths and hummingbirds.
One of the easiest and most reliable penstemons, with spires of deep blue-violet bells over glossy foliage. Tough, long-lived, and a bumblebee magnet for dry gardens.
A tall, dramatic penstemon and one of the few that's truly fragrant, with big inflated pink bells up gray-green stems. Thrives on hot, gravelly, alkaline ground.
Slender wands of scarlet tubular flowers with a bearded lower lip bloom into summer, built for hummingbird pollination. A brilliant, drought-tough mountain wildflower.
A low evergreen mat of spoon-shaped leaves topped by sulfur-yellow flower umbels that age to rust. An exceptional pollinator and butterfly-host plant for hot, dry, rocky sites.
A meadow and aspen-grove geranium with rich rose-purple, dark-veined flowers over deeply cut leaves. Long-blooming and a favorite of native bees.
Airy stems open a fresh succession of sky-blue, five-petaled flowers each morning through early summer. An effortless, drought-proof reseeder for sunny western gardens.
Clusters of nodding, bell-shaped flowers open pink and turn sky-blue along mountain streams and seeps. A lush, cool-loving perennial for moist shade and bog gardens.
Showy lavender-blue daisies with golden centers cover this bushy fleabane through summer in aspen meadows. Easy, floriferous, and beloved by a wide range of pollinators.
Silvery, silk-haired foliage sets off spikes of blue-purple pea flowers across the mountain West. A nitrogen-fixing meadow lupine and host plant for blue butterflies.
The wild blue flag of western mountain meadows, opening pale lavender-blue flowers veined in violet where snowmelt lingers. Spreads into colonies in seasonally wet ground.
Huge, fragrant white cups open at dusk from a low rosette and fade to pink by morning, drawing hawkmoths. A tough, stemless wildflower for hot, dry, gravelly ground.
Dainty, nodding blue bells dance on thread-fine stems all summer across rocky slopes and meadows. A delicate, wide-ranging wildflower, the bluebell of Scotland, that tucks into crevices.
Bold yellow sunflowers rise over big silvery arrowhead leaves, gilding entire hillsides of the sagebrush steppe in spring. A deep taproot makes it drought-proof and long-lived.
Clusters of fragrant orange-to-yellow four-petaled flowers crown leafy stems in spring, lighting up rocky slopes and roadsides. An easy, cheerful reseeder for dry ground.
A low, gray-hairy sprawler that covers itself in small golden daisies from midsummer to frost on the harshest dry, gravelly ground. An unfussy, long-blooming pollinator plant.
The brilliant scarlet paintbrush of mountain meadows, its color coming from flame-tipped bracts rather than the hidden flowers. Hemiparasitic, drawing nutrients from neighboring plants' roots.
A towering, blue-green bunchgrass of valley bottoms and swales, with stiff wheat-like flower spikes. Bold structure and important cover and forage in the sagebrush country.
A delicate, airy bunchgrass whose open, beaded flower clouds shimmer over the driest sands. Its nutritious seeds were an important Indigenous grain, and it stabilizes desert dunes.
A fine-textured, blue-green bunchgrass of southwestern mountain meadows and ponderosa parklands, forming neat tufts. A drought-tolerant cool-season grass for high, dry sites.

A wide-spreading desert tree with weeping branches, fragrant cream catkins, and sweet pods long ground into meal. A nitrogen-fixer whose deep roots tap groundwater across the southern plains and deserts.
A slow, yellow-green-barked desert tree of rocky slopes, paler-flowered than its blue cousin and an essential nurse plant for young saguaros. Supremely drought-adapted.
A thorny desert legume with fragrant golden flower puffs after summer rains and ferny foliage. A nitrogen-fixer that anchors washes and provides cover and forage.
A sturdy evergreen oak of the Southwest's sky islands, with thick, leathery blue-green leaves and acorns that feed wildlife. A keystone of the region's oak-pine woodlands.
Instantly known for its checkered, alligator-skin bark, this rugged juniper of dry mountain slopes bears blue berry-cones for wildlife and resprouts after fire. Long-lived and picturesque.
A fast, conical conifer with silvery blue-green scale-like foliage and shaggy bark, native to canyon slopes of the Southwest. Drought- and heat-tough, and a popular windbreak and screen.
An adaptable, olive-relative shrub with early yellow-green flowers and blue-black fruit that birds love. Tough, multi-trunked, and easily shaped into a small tree or hedge.
A thorny southwestern locust draped with fragrant rose-pink pea-flower clusters in early summer. A nitrogen-fixer that suckers into thickets and feeds hummingbirds and bees.
The surreal, branching giant yucca that defines the Mojave Desert, raising clusters of creamy bells atop spiky arms. Pollinated solely by the yucca moth and slow-growing over centuries.
A mounding hedgehog cactus that lights up rocky slopes with brilliant scarlet, waxy cups, one of the few cacti pollinated by hummingbirds. Forms dense clumps of hundreds of stems with age.
A low prickly pear with blue-gray, beavertail-shaped pads that erupt in vivid magenta flowers in spring. Beware the tiny barbed glochids that replace the usual spines.
A tree-like cholla whose dense, barbed joints detach at the slightest brush, seeming to 'jump,' and chain into hanging fruit. A formidable, sculptural Sonoran giant that shelters cactus wrens.
A large gray-green rosette that, after years, sends up a towering flower stalk whose nectar sustains migrating lesser long-nosed bats. A keystone agave of the sky-island grasslands.
A stout, blue-green-bladed yucca bearing thick, banana-like fleshy fruit that was an important Indigenous food. Its fibers wove cordage and sandals, and the yucca moth pollinates its waxy bells.
A trunk-forming Mojave yucca with stiff, yellow-green blades edged in curling fibers and dense clusters of creamy bells. Tough, long-lived, and a desert habitat plant.
A ribbed cylinder armored in hooked red-and-yellow spines, crowned each spring by a ring of yellow-to-orange flowers. A long-lived sentinel of Mojave and Sonoran rocky slopes.
A massive blue-gray rosette of fierce, spine-tipped leaves that, after decades, throws up a twenty-foot flowering mast and dies, leaving offsets. Bold, dramatic, and bat- and bird-pollinated.
A prickly pear prized for rounded pads that flush purple and blue in cold or drought, set off by yellow flowers and red fruit. A living sculpture for hot, dry gardens.
A fast, lush desert shrub hung with clusters of golden trumpet flowers from spring to frost that hummingbirds and bees adore. Heat-loving and free-blooming wherever winters are mild.
An airy, fine-leaved shrub that flames with slender red-orange tubular flowers through the hottest months, a magnet for hummingbirds. A larval host and a tough late-season nectar plant.
A compact, silvery desert sage that mounds itself in spikes of violet-blue flowers wrapped in purple bracts. Intensely aromatic and built for the driest, rockiest Great Basin slopes.
A wispy desert shrub that after each rain perfumes the air with vanilla-scented white flower spikes swarming with bees and butterflies. An outstanding, drought-proof pollinator plant.
A tall, gray, lavender-scented desert shrub that blooms small violet flowers through the cool season. A vital winter nectar source for bees and hummingbirds in the Sonoran wash.
A fine-textured desert legume that covers itself in rich purple flower spikes in fall, alive with bees. A nitrogen-fixer and butterfly host that thrives on rocky, alkaline ground.
A low, silvery, trailing groundcover legume dotted with small purple flowers in spring. A fast, fine-textured nitrogen-fixer for covering hot, dry banks and slopes.
A spiny, blue-gray evergreen with holly-like leaflets, fragrant yellow late-winter flowers, and tart red berries gathered for jelly. Impenetrable cover for birds and tough on dry, limy soils.
A handsome, leathery-leaved evergreen of desert mountains, with flat clusters of creamy flowers and a narrow, upright form. A refined, drought-tough screen or small tree.
A rounded, glossy-leaved evergreen of chaparral and desert foothills with pinkish flower buds and tart reddish fruit once used for a sweet drink. A tough, fire-resistant screen.
A tidy, rounded evergreen with needle-fine, resin-scented foliage that bursts into golden daisies in fall, feeding late pollinators. Neat, fragrant, and unbothered by heat or drought.
A small shrub spangled through summer and fall with clusters of brilliant scarlet tubular flowers built for hummingbirds. A sky-island and desert-mountain gem for part shade.
A big, silvery, salt-loving evergreen shrub that forms dense thickets of cover and seed for quail and other birds. Thrives in saline, alkaline, and seasonally wet desert flats.
A low, gray, intricately branched shrub that, with creosote, dominates vast stretches of the warm deserts. An unshowy but essential nurse plant and soil-stabilizer of the open flats.
Tall, branching wands of rose-pink trumpets rise from clasping blue-gray leaves in spring, drawing hummingbirds across the desert. A showy, heat-loving penstemon for gravelly ground.
A tidy desert mound covered much of the year in small golden daisies whose rays dry to papery tan and persist on the plant. Cheerful, long-blooming, and thoroughly drought-proof.
A slender winter annual whose blue-purple flower spikes carpet the Sonoran Desert after good rains, often beside golden poppies. A nitrogen-fixer that reseeds for the next wet year.
A desert annual scattering deep gentian-blue bells over rounded, dark leaves in early spring. One of the truest blues of the Mojave bloom and a quick, easy reseeder.
A low, tough perennial zinnia forming tidy mounds dotted for months with small creamy-white daisies. An unfussy, heat- and drought-defying groundcover for the hottest gardens.
A low, spreading evening-primrose relative that opens a long succession of silky yellow cups aging to apricot, from spring into fall. Drought-tough and free-flowering on lean soil.
A wiry, rhizomatous warm-season grass that holds the soil across the driest shortgrass and desert grasslands, greening up fast after rain. Tough, important forage and erosion control.
A delicate, intricately branched bunchgrass that forms an airy purple haze in flower, often sheltering within desert shrubs. Nutritious forage and a beautiful, fine-textured accent.
A fast, towering lowland fir with flat, glossy needles in neat rows that release a bright citrus scent when crushed. A stately conifer for moist Northwest valleys.
The largest of the true firs, a high-elevation conifer with stiff blue-green needles and huge upright cones. Prized as a premium Christmas tree and timber, but it needs cool mountain air.
Idaho's state tree, a graceful five-needled pine with long banana-curved cones and soft blue-green foliage. A fast, tall conifer of mountain forests, though challenged by blister rust.
A deciduous conifer that turns whole mountainsides gold before dropping its soft needles each fall. Thick-barked and remarkably fire-resistant, a giant of the inland Northwest forests.
A fast pioneer tree of streambanks and clearings with smooth gray bark and dangling catkins. A nitrogen-fixer that enriches the soil and feeds siskins and redpolls with its tiny cone seeds.
The towering cottonwood of western rivers, balsam-scented at leaf-out and shedding drifts of cottony seed in summer. The tallest western hardwood and vital riparian habitat.

A slow, shade-loving understory conifer with flat needles, peeling purple bark, and red berry-like arils for birds. The original source of the cancer drug taxol, though its foliage and seeds are toxic.
A thorny, thicket-forming hawthorn with flat white flower clusters and dark, edible haws. Impenetrable cover and abundant food for nesting and foraging birds along moist edges.
A graceful, weeping conifer of cold, wet mountain slopes, with drooping sprays of blue-green foliage and aromatic, rot-resistant yellow wood. Long-lived and elegant in the landscape.
A moisture-loving shrub bearing paired yellow tubular flowers for hummingbirds, then shiny black twin berries cupped in showy maroon bracts that birds relish. Vigorous in wet ground.
An airy, green-twigged huckleberry of conifer forests, often growing from rotting stumps, hung with translucent bright-red berries beloved by birds and people. Delicate and graceful in shade.
The famous mountain huckleberry, producing large, sweet purple-black berries treasured by people and bears alike. Thrives in cool, high forests and burns, with rich red fall color.
A suckering wetland shrub topped in summer with fuzzy pink flower spires (hardhack) that bees and butterflies swarm. Excellent for soggy ground, rain gardens, and stabilizing banks.
A deciduous, red-stemmed wild lilac that froths with fragrant white flower clusters in early summer. A nitrogen-fixing fire-follower beloved by butterflies and native bees on dry forest edges.
A glossy, aromatic evergreen of the coast with purple waxy berries for birds. Fast, salt-tolerant, and a nitrogen-fixer, making an excellent screen or hedge in mild Northwest gardens.
A delicate, slender-stemmed little woodland rose with small single pink blooms and narrow red hips. The most shade-tolerant native rose, at home beneath conifers.
The native black raspberry, with arching blue-white canes and sweet, dusky purple-black fruit prized for fresh eating and jam. A vigorous, thorny thicket-former of clearings and edges.
The West's only deciduous native azalea, perfuming streamsides with showy white-to-pink, yellow-blotched flowers in early summer. Spectacular but wanting cool, moist, acidic roots.
A tough, mat-forming evergreen groundcover (bearberry) with glossy leaves, pink urn flowers, and red berries for wildlife. Superb for covering hot, dry, sandy banks and slopes.
A low, fine-textured evergreen with tiny glossy boxwood-like leaves and inconspicuous maroon flowers. A tidy, drought-tolerant native substitute for boxwood in sun or shade.
A creeping woodland groundcover dogwood that carpets cool conifer shade with whorled leaves, white flower bracts, and clusters of red berries. Spreads slowly into a lush mat.

A huckleberry look-alike (hence 'fool's') of moist mountain forests, with clusters of small salmon-pink urn flowers and sticky, skunky-scented foliage that colors well in fall. For cool, acidic shade.
Clusters of papery, pearl-white flower heads top silvery stems all summer and dry beautifully for arrangements. A tough pioneer of dry, disturbed ground and a host plant for painted lady butterflies.
A sprawling, late-blooming aster studding moist meadows and coastlines with violet-blue, gold-centered daisies. A vital fall nectar source that thrives from bog edges to beach bluffs.
A charming evergreen succulent forming silvery-blue rosettes, often blushed pink, topped with bright yellow star flowers. A carefree mat for rock gardens, walls, and green roofs.
A glossy-leaved, evergreen wild strawberry of coastal bluffs and dunes, with white flowers, small sweet berries, and runners that knit a tough groundcover. An ancestor of the cultivated strawberry.
A lush groundcover for damp shade, carpeting the forest floor with glossy heart-shaped leaves and fluffy spikes of white flowers, then speckled red berries. Spreads vigorously by rhizome.
Distinctive three-part fan-shaped leaves carpet cool conifer shade, sending up slender white flower spikes in spring. The dried foliage smells of vanilla and was hung as an insect repellent.
Delicate, duck-foot-shaped leaflets and sprays of tiny white flowers whose swept-back petals look turned inside out. An airy, spreading groundcover for moist woodland shade.
The Northwest's wild tiger lily, nodding whorls of recurved orange flowers spotted maroon over meadows and forest edges. A striking native lily that draws swallowtails and hummingbirds.
Nodding, mottled brown-and-green bells dangle over grassy meadows in spring, then vanish by summer. A subtle, intriguing native lily of bluffs and oak woodlands.
Bright golden, swept-back flowers carpet mountain meadows at the very edge of melting snow, then go dormant. An early-spring ephemeral whose bulbs feed bears and ground squirrels.
A low, mat-forming penstemon clinging to cliffs and rock crevices, smothered in deep rose-pink trumpets in early summer. A jewel for rock gardens and crevice plantings.
Brushy spikes tipped with flame-red bracts color Northwest prairies and bluffs in spring. Like other paintbrushes it's hemiparasitic, tapping the roots of grasses and lupines nearby.
A dainty spring annual with bicolored flowers, white-to-violet above and deep blue below, scattered across rocky slopes and balds. An easy, charming reseeder for the spring meadow.
A glossy, plump-leaved evergreen succulent that reddens in sun and lifts bright yellow star flowers in summer. A tough, spreading mat for rock crevices, walls, and green roofs.
Lobed, marbled evergreen leaves form neat mounds beneath airy clouds of tiny white flowers on wiry stems. A rugged native coral bells for shaded rock gardens and the parent of many hybrids.
A winter-green fern that often grows on mossy logs and bigleaf maple trunks, going summer-dormant. Its sweet, licorice-flavored rhizomes were chewed by Indigenous peoples.
A lacy, finely cut wood fern forming graceful shuttlecock clumps in cool, moist conifer forests. An elegant, easy native for shaded woodland beds and stream edges.
A fine-textured, blue-green bunchgrass that anchors the Northwest's prairies and oak savannas. A tidy, drought-tolerant cool-season grass for meadows and well-drained gardens.
The common scrub oak of the southern Rockies and Colorado Plateau, forming thickets that blaze orange in fall. Its acorns feed deer, turkeys, and bears, and it resprouts vigorously after fire.
The 'paradox' coneflower, the only Echinacea with yellow rather than purple rays, drooping golden petals around a dark cone. A tough Ozark glade native and a pollinator favorite.
A fine, threadleaf ironweed forming a soft mound topped with clusters of vivid violet flowers in late summer. The compact, mildew-free 'Iron Butterfly' selection comes from this Ozark species.
A rare, federally threatened, rosemary-scented evergreen of Cumberland riverbanks, forming low mats studded with lavender flowers. A fragrant, drought-tough groundcover for hot, sandy sites.
A delicate, fine-leaved blazing star with slender wands of rosy-purple flowers in late summer over grassy foliage. A tidy, drought-tolerant native and a magnet for butterflies.
Silvery furry catkins emerge in late winter, a prized cut stem and one of the earliest pollen sources for native bees. A multi-stemmed wetland shrub for moist soils and pond edges.
A thicket-forming streambank willow with narrow silvery leaves, spreading by root suckers to bind soil. Excellent for erosion control and streambank restoration across the West and Great Plains.
An upland willow of western forests, more drought-tolerant than most, often colonizing after fire. Early catkins feed emerging bees and butterflies; foliage supports abundant caterpillars.
Named for its glossy, lustrous leaves, this wetland willow forms a handsome large shrub or small tree along streams and marsh edges in the North and East.
The common riparian willow of California and the Southwest, anchoring arroyos and creek banks. Among the very first plants to bloom, fueling early-season pollinators.

The iconic white-barked paper birch, its peeling chalky bark a year-round feature. Best in cool climates with moist soil; catkins and seeds feed redpolls and siskins, foliage feeds hundreds of caterpillars.
A long-lived northern hardwood with shining bronze-gold curling bark and wintergreen-scented twigs. Prefers cool, moist, rich soils and tolerates more shade than other birches.
Sweet birch has dark cherry-like bark and aromatic wintergreen twigs that were once the source of birch beer. Glowing yellow fall color and adaptability to drier upland sites set it apart.
A fast, short-lived pioneer birch that thrives on poor sandy and disturbed soils where little else will grow. Slender chalky-gray trunks often lean and cluster, with fluttering aspen-like leaves.
Water birch is a multi-stemmed western shrub or small tree of mountain streamsides, with shiny reddish-bronze, non-peeling bark. Valuable for riparian plantings in the Rockies and Great Basin.
Bigtooth aspen spreads into golden-leaved groves by root suckers, a fast pioneer on sandy, disturbed ground. Coarse-toothed leaves flutter on flattened stalks and turn clear gold in fall.

The great shade tree of Southwestern desert rivers, Fremont cottonwood marks the line of permanent water. Its broad canopy and golden fall foliage anchor cottonwood-willow riparian habitat prized by wildlife.
Narrowleaf cottonwood reads almost like a willow, with slender pale-green leaves lining mountain streams across the interior West. A key riparian tree at higher elevations than Fremont cottonwood.
The northernmost American hardwood, balsam poplar fills spring air with the sweet balsam scent of its resinous, sticky buds. A fast, cold-hardy riparian tree of the boreal North.
A low, sprawling cherry of dunes, shores, and rocky barrens, smothered in white bloom each spring. Tough, salt- and drought-tolerant groundcover with edible dark cherries.
Pin cherry is a fast, short-lived pioneer that floods burned and cleared land with white bloom, then bright red cherries beloved by birds. Its quick shade nurses the next forest.

Chickasaw plum forms suckering thickets that foam white in earliest spring before the leaves, then ripen sweet red-and-yellow plums. A classic wildlife thicket and edible hedgerow of the South and Plains.

Bitter cherry is a slender western cherry of foothills and forest openings, clouded with fragrant white flowers and hung with small bitter-red fruit that birds devour. An important larval host in the West.
Beach plum is the salt- and sand-tolerant shrub of Atlantic dunes, blanketed in white bloom then heavy with purple plums famous for jam. Among the toughest natives for seaside gardens.
Unlike its suckering kin, Mexican plum grows as a single-trunked small tree, making it a tidy specimen for small yards. Fragrant white spring bloom, ripe purple plums, and good drought tolerance.
The only native plum of California and southern Oregon, Klamath plum forms thorny thickets bright with white bloom and edible red-purple plums long gathered for jelly.
Cockspur hawthorn spreads in stiff horizontal tiers armed with long thorns, making an impenetrable wildlife hedge. White spring flowers give way to red haws that hold into winter for birds.
Green hawthorn tolerates wet ground and city conditions, carrying clusters of white bloom and a heavy crop of glossy red fruit that persists through winter for cedar waxwings and robins.
Washington hawthorn blooms late, after most trees have leafed out, then sets shining scarlet haws against fiery orange-red fall foliage. A favorite small specimen and screening tree.
Downy hawthorn is the largest of the Midwestern hawthorns, with soft fuzzy leaves, broad clusters of white bloom, and unusually large red fruit relished by wildlife.
Shadblow serviceberry blooms in a haze of white as the shad run upriver, then ripens sweet blue-purple berries in June. A suckering, multi-stemmed shrub for moist sites with fine orange fall color.
Allegheny serviceberry unfurls bronze-tinted new leaves beneath drooping white bloom, sets edible blue berries, and finishes in brilliant orange-red. One of the best four-season small native trees.

Beaked hazelnut is an adaptable understory shrub whose long male catkins dangle through winter and open before the leaves. Its nuts, wrapped in a distinctive bristly beak, feed jays, grouse, and squirrels.

Sweetfern is a low, aromatic shrub with soft fern-like foliage that releases a sweet resinous scent. A nitrogen-fixer for the poorest dry, sandy, acidic soils, spreading into deer-resistant colonies for erosion control.

Sweet crabapple perfumes the late-spring woods with intensely fragrant pink-and-white bloom, followed by hard green crabapples used for jelly and eaten by wildlife. A broad, often thorny native orchard tree.

Prairie crabapple is the wild crabapple of the tallgrass Midwest, opening showy fragrant pink blossoms a little later than its eastern cousin. Tart green fruit makes good jelly and feeds wildlife through fall.

Pacific crabapple, the only crabapple native to the West Coast, thrives in wet thickets and stream edges from California to Alaska. Fragrant white bloom yields tiny tart apples long harvested by coastal peoples.

Allegheny chinquapin is a shrubby relative of the American chestnut, far more resistant to chestnut blight, that bears small, sweet, edible nuts in spiny burs. Thrives on dry, sandy, acidic upland soils.

Riverbank grape is a vigorous, cold-hardy native vine that drapes streamside trees and fences, its tiny fragrant flowers giving way to tart blue-black grapes. Heavy fruit and dense cover make it a wildlife magnet; give it room.

California wild grape is a robust riparian vine celebrated for its blazing crimson and gold fall color, with small sweet-tart grapes for birds and people. Drought-tolerant once established; vigorous enough for arbors and screening.
Winged elm is a tough, adaptable Southern elm named for the corky wings that ride its twigs. Small and drought-hardy, it tolerates poor soils where larger elms fail and supports many caterpillars.
Cedar elm is the iconic shade tree of Texas and the south-central states, thriving on heavy alkaline clay and drought. Unusually, it blooms and seeds in late summer or fall rather than spring.

American mountain-ash brightens cool northern woods and mountainsides with flat clusters of white bloom and, by fall, blazing orange-red berries that waxwings and grosbeaks strip quickly. Needs cool, moist, acidic soil.
A small red oak of sandy uplands and stream terraces in the Gulf states, with glossy leaves and a tidy rounded crown. Acorns feed turkey, deer, and squirrels; foliage hosts hundreds of caterpillars.

Swamp white oak is one of the best native shade trees for wet or poorly drained ground, with lustrous two-toned leaves and handsome peeling bark. Fast-growing, long-lived, and a powerhouse caterpillar host.

Texas red oak brings reliable scarlet fall color to the limestone hills of central Texas, thriving on dry, rocky, alkaline soils where larger oaks struggle. Drought-tough and well scaled for smaller yards.

Chapman oak is a shrubby evergreen oak of Florida and Gulf-coast scrub, holding leathery leaves through winter on deep, dry sand. A key component of imperiled scrub habitat and a host for scrub-specialist wildlife.
Canyon live oak is a sturdy evergreen oak of western canyons and slopes, with hard wood and golden-fuzzed young acorns. Tolerates rocky ground and shade, anchoring foothill and montane woodlands.

Coastal sage scrub oak is a low evergreen oak of southern California's coastal chaparral, forming dense thickets that shelter birds and small mammals. Drought-hardy and valuable for restoring rare scrub habitat.
Northern pin oak thrives on dry, sandy, acidic soils of the upper Midwest, lighting up in deep red each fall. A tough, fast red oak for poor ground where many shade trees fail.

Engelmann oak is a rare, broad-crowned oak of southern California's inland valleys and foothills, semi-evergreen with blue-green leaves. A signature tree of vanishing oak savanna, prized and increasingly protected.
Southern red oak is a large, drought-tolerant upland oak of the Southeast, with deeply cut, bell-based leaves and a spreading crown. A fast-growing shade tree and heavy acorn producer for wildlife.

Escarpment live oak is the cold- and drought-hardy live oak of the Texas Hill Country, evergreen and immensely tough on thin limestone soils. Spreads into picturesque mottes that define the central Texas landscape.
Gray oak is a small, gray-leaved oak of Southwestern mountains and high desert, often shrubby on rocky slopes. Drought-adapted and an important acorn and host resource in arid uplands.

Havard shin oak is a knee-high, rhizomatous oak that binds the deep sands of the Southern Plains into vast clonal 'shinnery' colonies. Ecologically vital for sand-dune stabilization and the lesser prairie-chicken.
Darlington oak is a fast, semi-evergreen laurel-leaved oak of Southeastern sandy uplands, holding glossy foliage well into winter. A popular tough street and shade tree across the lower South.
Bear oak is a thicket-forming scrub oak of Northeastern barrens and rocky ridgetops, thriving where soils are too poor and dry for most trees. Heavy acorn crops feed birds and mammals through winter.

Shingle oak is unusual among oaks for its smooth, unlobed laurel-like leaves, many held coppery through winter. Adaptable and tolerant of pruning, it makes an excellent large hedge or screen as well as a shade tree.
Bluejack oak is a small sandhill oak of the Southeast, named for the blue-gray cast of its narrow leaves. A drought-hardy host tree of deep, dry sands and longleaf-pine sandhills.

Lacey oak is a refined small oak of the Texas Hill Country limestone, with soft blue-green leaves that emerge peach-pink. Drought- and heat-proof, it is one of the best native shade trees for small alkaline-soil gardens.

Turkey oak is the characteristic oak of Southeastern sandhills, with large, three-lobed leaves that turn red in fall and twist to face the sun. Thrives on the driest, deepest sands alongside longleaf pine.
Laurel oak is a fast-growing, semi-evergreen oak of Southeastern bottomlands and stream edges, holding glossy leaves nearly year-round. A quick shade producer that pours out acorns for wildlife.

Blackjack oak is a small, rugged oak that colonizes the poorest dry clay and sand, with distinctive broad, bell-shaped leaves and blackish bark. Exceptionally drought-tough and a reliable wildlife oak on hardpan soils.

Swamp chestnut oak, also called basket oak, is a stately bottomland white oak with large, scalloped leaves and big, sweet acorns favored by deer and turkey. A superb long-lived shade tree for moist, rich soils.

Mohr oak is a thicket-forming shin oak of West Texas and Southwestern limestone outcrops, with leathery, gray-backed evergreen leaves. Drought- and lime-tolerant cover and acorn source in arid country.

Myrtle oak is a compact evergreen scrub oak with small, glossy, rolled-edged leaves, native to Florida and Gulf-coast dune scrub. A drought- and salt-tolerant component of rare coastal scrub communities.

Cherrybark oak is a magnificent, fast-growing red oak of rich Southern bottomlands, with cherry-like scaly bark and a tall straight trunk. One of the finest large shade and timber oaks, and a heavy acorn producer.

Bastard oak, or Durand oak, is an adaptable white-group oak ranging from a shrub on dry limestone to a sizable tree on better soils. Drought-tolerant and useful where alkaline ground limits other oaks.

Post oak is a slow, immensely tough white oak with cross-shaped leaves, dominating dry, poor upland soils from the Mid-Atlantic to Texas. Long-lived and drought-proof, though it resents root disturbance.

Nuttall oak is a fast-growing bottomland red oak that tolerates heavy, wet clay and seasonal flooding, finishing the year in red and orange. An increasingly popular tough shade tree with reliable fall color.
Sonoran scrub oak is a spiny-leaved evergreen oak of Southwestern chaparral and desert mountains, forming dense drought-proof thickets. Critical cover and acorn mast for desert birds and mammals.
Vasey shin oak is a small, often clumping oak of the Edwards Plateau limestone, with wavy-edged semi-evergreen leaves. Heat- and drought-hardy cover and acorn source for Hill Country wildlife.

Interior live oak is a dense, dark evergreen oak of California's foothills and interior ranges, tolerating heat, drought, and rocky ground. A year-round shelter and acorn tree central to oak-woodland wildlife.
The southern counterpart of sugar maple, Florida maple is a heat-tolerant shade tree with golden-orange fall color for the lower South. Smaller and more drought-adapted than its northern kin.

Rocky Mountain maple is a graceful multi-stemmed small maple of western mountain canyons and streamsides, with reddish twigs and clear yellow fall color. Tough and cold-hardy for high-elevation gardens.
Chalk maple is a refined understory maple named for its pale, smooth bark, lighting up shaded Southeastern slopes with orange and red fall color. An excellent small native shade tree for tight spots.

Boxelder is a fast, adaptable maple with compound leaves, thriving on floodplains and disturbed ground from coast to coast. Its abundant seeds feed the evening grosbeak and the boxelder bug through winter.
Black maple is a close cousin of sugar maple with drooping, dark green leaves and the same brilliant yellow-orange fall display. A long-lived shade and syrup tree tolerant of heavier, drier soils.

Striped maple, or moosewood, is a shade-loving understory tree prized for its green bark vertically striped in white. A cool-woodland specimen with big, three-lobed leaves turning soft yellow in fall.
Mountain maple is a small, shrubby understory maple of cool northern forests, the only native maple with upright flower spikes. Brilliant red-and-yellow fall color brightens shaded, rocky slopes.
Water hickory is a tall bottomland hickory of Southern swamps and river floodplains, tolerant of prolonged flooding. Its bitter nuts feed wood ducks and mammals where few other hickories grow.

Pecan is the largest of the hickories and the source of the beloved native nut, a massive, long-lived shade tree of river bottoms. Needs room and rich, deep soil; rewards with shade, mast, and harvest.
Black hickory is a drought-hardy upland hickory of dry, sandy, and rocky soils across the south-central states. A tough wildlife tree where richer-soil hickories cannot persist.
Mockernut hickory is a sturdy, deep-rooted upland hickory with fragrant, fuzzy foliage that turns rich gold in fall and thick-shelled, sweet nuts. Long-lived and drought-tolerant on dry ridges.

Southern California walnut is a small, often multi-trunked walnut of southern California foothills and washes, anchoring a rare walnut-woodland habitat. Drought-deciduous and valuable for wildlife mast.
Butternut, or white walnut, is a fast, spreading tree of cool moist woods bearing sweet, oily, oblong nuts. Increasingly rare due to butternut canker, making conservation plantings valuable.
Arizona walnut is the shade tree of Southwestern canyon streams and desert riparian corridors, marking water in arid country. Its small hard nuts sustain squirrels and other wildlife.
Little walnut is a small, often shrubby walnut of Texas and Southwestern streambeds and limestone draws, with tiny thick-shelled nuts. Heat- and drought-tough for arid riparian plantings.

The American chestnut was once the dominant canopy tree of eastern forests before chestnut blight; surviving sprouts and blight-resistant breeding keep it alive. Plant as part of restoration, knowing blight remains a risk.
Sugarberry is a fast, tough Southern shade tree closely related to hackberry, with smooth gray bark and sweet berries devoured by birds. Thrives on bottomlands and in tough urban sites alike.

Honey locust casts light, dappled shade through ferny compound leaves, with fragrant spring flowers and long sweet seed pods relished by wildlife. Extremely tough on poor, dry, or alkaline urban soils; wild trees bear formidable thorns.
Arizona sycamore is the grand, white-barked shade tree of Southwestern canyon streams, its mottled trunk and huge lobed leaves marking permanent water. A keystone of desert riparian woodland.

Ogeechee tupelo is a swamp tree of the Southeastern coastal plain whose nectar yields the famed, never-crystallizing tupelo honey. Its tart red fruit feeds wildlife in the blackwater swamps it calls home.

Texas ash is a compact, drought-hardy ash of central Texas limestone hills with reliable purple-and-gold fall color. Like all ashes, it is vulnerable to the emerald ash borer, which is spreading west — plant with that risk in mind.

White ash is a stately native shade tree with rich purple fall color and a powerhouse caterpillar host. The emerald ash borer has devastated wild populations, so new plantings should be sited and monitored accordingly.

Mexican (Berlandier) ash is a fast, heat-tolerant ash of south Texas and the Rio Grande, popular for quick shade. As with all ashes, the emerald ash borer poses a serious long-term threat to consider before planting.

Carolina ash, or pop ash, is a small Southeastern swamp ash tolerant of standing water, with conspicuous winged seeds. A wetland wildlife tree — though, like all ashes, at risk from the emerald ash borer.

Gregg's ash is a small, fine-textured evergreen-tending ash of Southwestern limestone canyons, well suited to xeric gardens. Far more drought-adapted than its large cousins, though still an emerald ash borer host.

Oregon ash is the West Coast's native ash, a fast bottomland and streamside tree turning clear yellow in fall. The emerald ash borer has now reached the Pacific Northwest, so plant and monitor with care.

Black ash is a slow, wetland ash of northern swamps and fens, culturally vital for traditional basketry. It is among the most threatened by the emerald ash borer, lending conservation urgency to its planting.

Green ash is a fast, adaptable native ash once planted everywhere for quick shade, with golden fall color and abundant wildlife seed. The emerald ash borer has hit it hard, so weigh that risk against its toughness.

Velvet (Arizona) ash is a drought-tolerant Southwestern ash of desert canyons and a popular irrigated shade tree, with velvety twigs and yellow fall color. As with all ashes, consider emerald ash borer risk before planting.
Knobcone pine is a fire-dependent western pine whose tightly sealed cones cling for decades and open only in a blaze. A pioneer of poor, rocky, fire-prone slopes from Oregon to Baja.
Mexican pinyon is a compact, drought-hardy pine of Southwestern highlands bearing large, sweet, edible pine nuts. Slow and long-lived, ideal for low-water gardens on lean soils.
Sand pine thrives on the deepest, driest white sands of Florida scrub where almost nothing else grows. A fast, short-lived pine central to the rare and fire-shaped scrub ecosystem.
Coulter pine produces the heaviest cones of any pine — spiny, ten-pound 'widow-makers' — on dry California mountain slopes. A bold, drought-tough specimen for large dry gardens.
Shortleaf pine is the most widespread Southern yellow pine, valued for timber and able to resprout after fire. A fast, adaptable evergreen for dry upland soils across the Southeast.
Slash pine is a fast-growing Southeastern pine of flatwoods and pond margins, tolerant of wet feet and salt air. A major timber and wildlife pine, with seeds eaten by many birds.
Apache pine is a long-needled pine of Southwestern 'sky island' mountains, its drooping foot-long needles giving a striking, almost tropical look. Fire-adapted and drought-hardy at elevation.
Spruce pine is an unusual shade-tolerant Southern pine of moist bottomlands, with soft needles and smooth bark unlike its rugged kin. A graceful evergreen for partly shaded, damp sites.
Sugar pine is the tallest and largest of all pines, towering over western mountain forests with cones up to two feet long. A magnificent, long-lived giant for spacious cool-climate sites.
Table Mountain pine is a rugged, fire-dependent pine of dry Appalachian ridgetops, armed with stout-spined cones that persist for years. Tolerant of the poorest rocky, acidic ground.
Monterey pine is a fast-growing pine native to a few fog-bathed stretches of the California coast, though planted worldwide for timber. Best near the coast where summers stay cool and moist.
Red pine is a clean, straight-trunked northern pine with reddish plated bark and long paired needles. Cold-hardy and tolerant of dry, sandy soils across the upper Midwest and Northeast.
Pitch pine is the tough, irregular pine of Northeastern barrens and bogs, uniquely able to resprout from its trunk after fire. Thrives on the poorest sand, gravel, and rock.

Pond pine is a fire- and flood-adapted pine of Southeastern pocosins and wet flatwoods, with closed cones that open after fire and sprouts that flush from the trunk. A specialist of saturated, acidic ground.
Southwestern white pine is a soft, five-needled pine of Southwestern mountains, bearing large wingless seeds prized by nutcrackers and jays. A handsome, drought-tolerant high-country pine.
Loblolly pine is the fast-growing workhorse pine of the Southeast, dominating old fields and timberland alike. Adaptable to wet or dry soils, it pours out seed for birds and small mammals.
Engelmann spruce is the tall, spire-like spruce of high western mountains, forming dark subalpine forests near treeline. Cold-loving and best in cool, moist climates at elevation.
White spruce is a hardy, adaptable northern spruce forming the backbone of the boreal forest, with dense conical form and short blue-green needles. Tolerant of cold, wind, and varied soils.
Red spruce is the fragrant spruce of cool Appalachian highlands and northern forests, shade-tolerant when young and slow to mature. A signature tree of high-elevation spruce-fir habitat.
Tamarack is North America's deciduous conifer, its soft needles flushing bright green in spring and turning glowing gold before dropping in fall. A bog and muskeg tree for cold, wet, acidic ground.
Balsam fir is the classic fragrant Christmas-tree fir of the northern forest, with flat aromatic needles and upright purple cones. Needs cool, moist, acidic conditions; not a heat-lover.
Subalpine fir is the narrow, steeple-crowned fir of high western mountains, shedding snow with its tight spire. A cold-climate specimen for elevation and cool, moist sites.
Mountain hemlock is a graceful, blue-green hemlock of snowy western high country, with nodding leaders and soft, plush foliage. A cool-climate evergreen for moist, well-drained mountain sites.

Bigcone Douglas-fir is a drought-hardy southern California endemic with the largest cones of any Douglas-fir, clinging to dry chaparral mountainsides. A tough, fire-resistant evergreen for hot, dry slopes.
Common juniper is the widest-ranging conifer on Earth, a low spreading shrub of rocky, open ground whose aromatic blue berries flavor gin. Tough, deer-resistant, and superb for dry slopes.
Creeping juniper is a hardy, mat-forming evergreen groundcover that hugs the ground over rock, sand, and slopes, often bronzing in winter. A go-to deer-resistant cover for hot, dry, exposed sites.
One-seed juniper is a rugged, often multi-trunked juniper of Southwestern high desert and grassland, immensely drought-tough. Its berries feed wintering birds across the arid Southwest.
Western juniper is the gnarled, ancient-looking juniper of the Great Basin and eastern Cascades, some specimens centuries old. A picturesque, drought-proof evergreen for harsh dry country.
Utah juniper is the dominant juniper of the Intermountain West's pinyon-juniper woodlands, squat and broad on dry rocky ground. Critical winter forage and cover for desert wildlife.
Redberry juniper is a Southwestern juniper distinctive for its coral-red berries, forming thickets on dry limestone and caprock. A drought-hardy, bird-feeding shrub for hot, lean soils.
Pond cypress is the slender, upright cousin of bald cypress, with feathery ascending foliage and a love of acidic ponds and pocosins. Turns rusty bronze in fall before dropping its needles.
Montezuma cypress is a massive, fast-growing riparian cypress of the Rio Grande and south, nearly evergreen in mild climates. A grand shade tree for streamsides and large wet gardens.
Oklahoma plum, or sand plum, is a low thicket-forming plum of dry sandy and rocky prairies, white with bloom in earliest spring. Its small red plums are gathered for jelly and feed abundant wildlife.
Canada plum is a hardy northern plum bearing showy white-to-pink blossoms before the leaves and tart red plums good for preserves. A cold-tough small tree for hedgerows and wildlife plantings.
Flatwoods plum, or hog plum, is a small Southeastern plum that clouds dry woods and fencerows with white bloom in late winter. Its purple plums make fine jelly and draw birds and mammals.
Peachleaf willow is a graceful streamside willow tree with glossy, peach-like leaves, lining rivers across the Plains and West. Early catkins feed emerging pollinators along the water's edge.
Arctic willow is a ground-hugging dwarf shrub of tundra and alpine fellfields, one of the northernmost woody plants on Earth. A curiosity and rock-garden groundcover for cold climates.
Bebb willow is an adaptable upland-to-wetland willow, the source of 'diamond willow' wood, important browse and an early pollen source. A pioneering shrub of clearings, fens, and forest edges.
Coastal plain willow is the common streambank and marsh willow of the Southeast, fast-growing and quick to stabilize wet, disturbed ground. Early bloom fuels native bees along Southern waterways.
Prairie willow is unusual among willows for thriving in dry, open uplands rather than wetlands. A compact shrub whose early catkins are a critical first pollen source on prairies and barrens.
Bog birch is a low wetland birch of fens, bogs, and calcareous wet meadows, with small round leaves and a dense twiggy form. A cold-hardy host shrub for specialized wetland habitat.

Southern crabapple perfumes the spring woods with intensely fragrant rose-pink bloom, followed by aromatic green crabapples used for jelly and eaten by wildlife. A suckering small tree of the Southeast.
Speckled alder is a multi-stemmed wetland shrub of northern stream edges and swamps, fixing nitrogen and stabilizing banks. Its dangling catkins and tiny conelike fruit feed siskins and redpolls.
White alder is a fast riparian tree of western foothill streams, marking permanent water with its pale bark and glossy leaves. A nitrogen-fixing pioneer that shades and cools trout streams.

Hazel alder, or smooth alder, is a thicket-forming wetland shrub of Eastern streambanks and seeps, fixing nitrogen and armoring soil against erosion. Early catkins are among the first pollen of the year.
Yellow hawthorn is a small Southeastern hawthorn of dry sandy woods, white-flowered in spring with yellow to orange-red haws. A drought-hardy wildlife tree for lean upland soils.
Mayhaw is the beloved Southern wetland hawthorn whose bright red spring fruit makes the prized mayhaw jelly. A small bottomland tree that flowers very early and tolerates wet ground.
Littlehip hawthorn is a fine-twigged Southeastern hawthorn with small spoon-shaped leaves, clouds of tiny white flowers, and little red haws. A dense, thorny wildlife and hedge tree.
American red raspberry is the wild parent of the garden raspberry, forming arching, prickly canes hung with sweet red berries. Beloved by birds and people alike; spreads readily, so give it room.
Smooth rose is a nearly thornless prairie rose with fragrant pink blooms and red hips, spreading into low colonies. Tough and cold-hardy for meadows, slopes, and naturalistic borders.
Swamp rose is the native rose for wet ground, thriving in marshes and pond edges where most roses rot. Clear pink summer flowers and red hips make it a standout for rain gardens.
Virginia rose is a glossy-leaved native rose with single pink flowers, glowing red fall foliage, and persistent scarlet hips. Salt- and drought-tolerant, it excels in coastal and seaside gardens.
Running serviceberry is a low, suckering serviceberry that forms colonies on dry, sandy, or rocky ground, with white spring bloom and sweet blue-purple berries. A fine groundcovering wildlife shrub.
Utah serviceberry is the drought-hardy serviceberry of the Intermountain West, covering dry foothills in white bloom then dark edible berries. Important browse and forage in arid country.
Birchleaf spirea is a tidy, low western shrub topped with flat white flower clusters in summer and good orange-red fall color. Adaptable and compact for the front of borders and dry slopes.
Broadleaf meadowsweet is an Eastern wetland-edge shrub crowned with frothy pyramids of white-to-pale-pink summer bloom. A nectar magnet for pollinators in damp meadows and rain gardens.
Rosy spirea, or mountain spirea, brightens western mountain meadows and streamsides with fuzzy rose-pink flower clusters all summer. A compact, pollinator-rich shrub for cool, moist sites.

Greene's mountain ash is a western shrubby mountain-ash with ferny compound leaves, flat white flower clusters, and showy orange-red berry bunches that waxwings strip in fall. A cool-climate mountain shrub.

Mallow ninebark is a western ninebark with maple-like leaves, white-pink flower clusters, and shredding bark for winter interest. Drought-tolerant and quick to colonize dry slopes for erosion control.

Darrow's blueberry is a low evergreen blueberry of Southeastern pinelands with striking blue-green foliage, the heat-tolerant parent of Southern highbush varieties. Sweet small berries feed wildlife and people.
American cranberry is the trailing evergreen vine of acid bogs that yields the familiar tart red fruit. A creeping groundcover for permanently wet, sandy, acidic ground and rain-garden margins.
Shiny blueberry is a small, glossy-leaved evergreen blueberry that spreads into low colonies across Florida and Gulf-coast pinelands. Early bloom and sweet dark berries make it a wildlife staple.
Velvetleaf blueberry is a low, soft-haired wild blueberry of northern woods and barrens, prized for abundant sweet fruit. Cold-hardy and colony-forming on poor, acidic, sandy soils.
Small cranberry is a delicate, thread-stemmed evergreen creeper of cold sphagnum bogs, with nodding pink flowers and tart red berries. A specialist groundcover for saturated, acidic peat.
Bog bilberry is a low, blue-fruited bilberry of arctic and alpine heath and northern bogs, blazing red in fall. A cold-climate groundcover with sweet berries for wildlife and foragers.
Lingonberry is a glossy evergreen groundcover of northern and alpine heath, bearing tart red berries famous in Scandinavian cooking. A handsome, cold-hardy mat for acidic soils and rock gardens.
Bunchberry is a charming woodland groundcover, a dwarf dogwood whose whorled leaves cradle white dogwood 'flowers' followed by clusters of red berries. Carpets cool, moist, acidic shade.

Roughleaf dogwood is a tough, suckering shrub of prairie edges and fencerows, with flat white flower clusters and white berries that birds devour. Adaptable to dry, heavy soils for hedges and thickets.
Swamp dogwood, or stiff dogwood, is a wetland shrub of Southeastern stream edges and swamps, with white spring bloom and blue berries on red stalks. Excellent for rain gardens and pond margins.
Squashberry, or mooseberry, is a northern and montane viburnum with maple-like leaves and translucent red, tart edible fruit. A cold-hardy understory shrub with good red fall color.
Western viburnum is a Pacific Northwest woodland shrub with rounded leaves, white flower clusters, and blue-black berries for birds. An uncommon but garden-worthy native for part shade.

Hobblebush is a sprawling cool-forest viburnum whose low branches root where they touch, tripping the unwary. Big bold leaves, lacecap white bloom, and red-to-black fruit light up the shaded understory.

Nannyberry is a large, adaptable viburnum that can be grown as a tall shrub or small tree, with creamy flower clusters and sweet blue-black berries good for jelly. Tolerates shade, wet, and dry alike.

Walter's viburnum is a fine-textured Southeastern viburnum, semi-evergreen and shearable into hedges, smothered in tiny white flowers in early spring. Berries feed birds; tolerant of wet or dry sites.

American cranberrybush is the native highbush cranberry (distinct from the invasive European form), with lacecap white bloom, maple-like leaves, and edible scarlet fruit that persists into winter for birds.
Rusty blackhaw is a handsome small tree or large shrub with glossy leaves, rusty-fuzzed buds, white spring bloom, and waxy blue fruit. Drought-tough with superb burgundy fall color.
Alabama azalea bears clusters of lemon-scented white flowers with a yellow blotch, perfuming Southeastern woodland edges in spring. A graceful deciduous azalea for dappled, acidic shade.

Sweet azalea is a deciduous native azalea of Appalachian streamsides, prized for intensely fragrant white-to-blush flowers with showy red stamens in early summer and good fall color. A choice shrub for moist, acidic shade.
Coastal azalea is a low, stoloniferous deciduous azalea of the Atlantic coastal plain, with fragrant white-and-pink bloom and blue-green leaves. Spreads into drifts on sandy, acidic soil.

Great rhododendron, or rosebay, is the towering evergreen rhododendron that forms dense thickets in cool Appalachian forests, topped with trusses of white-to-rose bloom. A bold evergreen for moist, acidic shade.

Piedmont rhododendron is a compact evergreen rhododendron with scaly leaves and pink-to-lavender flowers, more sun- and heat-tolerant than its giant cousin. A versatile evergreen for acidic Southern gardens.
Roseshell azalea fills cool northern woods with clusters of clove-scented, clear pink flowers in late spring. A cold-hardy, fragrant deciduous azalea for moist, acidic, partly shaded sites.

Plumleaf azalea is a rare, late-summer-blooming azalea whose flaming red-orange flowers appear in July and August when little else blooms, drawing hummingbirds. A prized deciduous azalea for moist shade.
Black huckleberry is a low, colony-forming shrub of dry acidic woods and barrens, with reddish bell flowers and sweet, seedy black fruit. Brilliant crimson fall color and abundant wildlife value.
Box huckleberry is a rare evergreen groundcover huckleberry forming ancient, slowly creeping clonal colonies, some thousands of years old. A glossy, boxwood-like mat for acidic woodland shade.
Dwarf huckleberry is a low Southeastern huckleberry of pine flatwoods and bogs, with bristly bell flowers and edible black fruit. Spreads to form a sweet-fruited groundcover on acidic ground.
Sheep laurel is a low evergreen laurel of acidic bogs, barrens, and heath, with clusters of deep pink cup-shaped flowers. Tough and deer-resistant, though its foliage is toxic to livestock.
Western bog laurel is a dwarf evergreen laurel of western mountain bogs and wet meadows, studded with rose-purple flowers. A deer-resistant specialist for cold, wet, acidic ground.
Bog laurel is a slender northern evergreen laurel of sphagnum bogs and fens, with showy rose-pink spring flowers above blue-green leaves. Deer-resistant but toxic; a gem for cold acidic wetlands.

Arizona madrone is an evergreen madrone of Southwestern 'sky island' canyons, with smooth reddish bark, urn-shaped white flowers, and orange-red berries for birds. A drought-hardy small tree of the high desert mountains.
Texas madrone is a striking evergreen with sculptural, peeling pink-and-white bark, fragrant white bell flowers, and showy red berries. Slow but spectacular on dry limestone and rocky slopes.
Rusty staggerbush is an evergreen Florida shrub with rusty-scaled, wavy leaves and clusters of small white urn flowers. A drought- and deer-resistant component of scrub and pine flatwoods.
Coastal staggerbush is a small evergreen shrub of Florida flatwoods and bogs, with leathery rust-backed leaves and white bell flowers. Tolerant of wet and dry, and resistant to deer.
Piedmont staggerbush is a deciduous shrub of acidic sandy woods and pond edges, hung with showy nodding white-to-pink bell flowers in late spring. Spreads modestly and shrugs off deer browsing.

Tall goldenrod is the billowing goldenrod of old fields and roadsides, a critical late-season nectar and pollen source for migrating monarchs and native bees. Vigorous and rhizomatous — give it room or a wild meadow.

California goldenrod brings sprays of golden bloom to dry western slopes and grasslands in late summer, fueling pollinators when little else flowers. Drought-tolerant and well suited to low-water gardens.
Canada goldenrod is the classic plume goldenrod of meadows across the continent, a powerhouse of late-season nectar for bees and butterflies. Spreads aggressively by rhizome, ideal for naturalizing.
Pine barren goldenrod thrives in wet pine flatwoods, bogs, and ditches of the Southeast, lighting up damp ground with gold in fall. A standout late nectar plant for rain gardens and wet meadows.
Giant goldenrod, or smooth goldenrod, rises on smooth, waxy stems in moist meadows and stream edges, crowned with arching golden plumes. A robust pollinator magnet for damp, sunny ground.
Julia's goldenrod is a Southeastern goldenrod of open sandy woods and pinelands, offering the same brilliant fall color and late nectar that make goldenrods so valuable to pollinators.
Rocky Mountain goldenrod is a compact alpine and arctic goldenrod of high rocky meadows and tundra, topped with tight golden clusters. A cold-hardy front-of-border or rock-garden pollinator plant.
Roundleaf goldenrod is a wetland goldenrod of swamps, seeps, and wet woods, with distinctive rough, broad basal leaves. Tolerant of part shade and wet feet, it lights up shady damp corners in fall.
Downy goldenrod is a well-behaved, clump-forming goldenrod for dry, sandy, or rocky soils, with showy wand-like golden spikes. A tidier choice than the running goldenrods for borders.
Rough goldenrod is a drought-hardy goldenrod of dry prairies, glades, and rocky barrens, with sandpapery leaves and dense golden plumes. Reliable late-season fuel for pollinators on lean soils.

Northern bayberry is a tough, aromatic, salt-tolerant shrub whose waxy gray berries make bayberry candles and feed myrtle warblers through winter. A nitrogen-fixing, deer-resistant pick for coastal and poor sandy soils.

White chaparral currant brightens the southern California dry season with fragrant white flower clusters from winter into early spring, when pollinators most need them. Summer-deciduous and very drought-tolerant.

Chaparral currant is a fragrant California native that blooms in fall and winter with drooping pink flower clusters, feeding hummingbirds and bees in the lean months. Drought-deciduous and superb for dry shade.
Canyon gooseberry is a spiny California shrub hung with dramatic fuchsia-like red-and-white flowers that hummingbirds adore, followed by bristly edible berries. A wildlife gem for woodland edges.
Appalachian gooseberry is an eastern woodland gooseberry with small greenish flowers and smooth purple, edible berries relished by birds. A modest but useful understory shrub for moist shade.

Evergreen currant, or Catalina currant, is a low, spreading evergreen shrub for dry shade, with aromatic leathery leaves and small wine-red flowers. One of the best California natives for groundcover under oaks.

Woodland strawberry is a dainty groundcovering strawberry that spreads by runners through dappled woodland, offering white flowers and intensely flavored tiny red berries. A charming edible groundcover for part shade.

Wild strawberry is the native parent of the garden strawberry, a tough, fast groundcover spreading by runners with white flowers and sweet small berries beloved by wildlife. Excellent edible living mulch.

Bristly locust is a suckering shrub draped with showy rose-pink pea flowers in late spring, its stems clad in red bristles. A nitrogen-fixing bank-stabilizer for dry, poor soils; spreads enthusiastically.

Black locust drips with fragrant white pea-flower chains in spring, a premier honeybee tree, and fixes nitrogen on the poorest ground. Fast and tough, but it suckers aggressively and spreads beyond its Appalachian native range — site with care.
Ozark witch hazel unfurls fragrant, ribbon-petaled yellow-to-red flowers in the depths of winter, scenting the cold air when nothing else blooms. A suckering streambank shrub with golden fall color.

Thin-leaved sunflower is a woodland-edge perennial sunflower with pale yellow daisies on branching stems, thriving in part shade where few sunflowers will. Seeds feed finches; foliage hosts many caterpillars.

Ashy sunflower is a striking prairie sunflower clad in soft, silvery-gray clasping leaves, topped with bright yellow blooms in midsummer. Drought-tough and rhizomatous for dry meadows and naturalizing.
Western sunflower is unusual for its near-leafless flowering stalks rising from a basal rosette, lifting yellow daisies above dry prairies and glades. A tidy, drought-hardy sunflower for lean soils.
Prairie sunflower is a fast annual of sandy plains and dunes, bearing small classic sunflowers all summer and abundant seed for birds. Reseeds readily, quickly colonizing open, dry, disturbed ground.
Paleleaf sunflower is a tall, adaptable woodland sunflower with pale-backed leaves and cheerful yellow blooms, spreading by rhizome at forest edges. A robust nectar-and-seed source for shadier sites.

Prairie flameleaf sumac is a small Texas tree with ferny compound leaves that blaze scarlet in fall and upright clusters of fuzzy red fruit for birds. Drought-proof and ornamental for dry, alkaline soils.
Littleleaf sumac is a spiny, small-leaved desert shrub of the Southwest, with early flowers and tart red berries that wildlife depend on. Exceptionally drought- and heat-tolerant for xeric gardens.
Evergreen sumac is a lustrous, leathery-leaved shrub of Texas and Southwestern limestone hills, with late white bloom and red fruit. A handsome, drought-hardy evergreen screen that shrugs off heat.
Mountain whitethorn is a dense, spiny, nitrogen-fixing ceanothus of western mountain forests, frosting slopes with white bloom after snowmelt. A fire-following pollinator magnet for high, dry ground.
Buckbrush is one of the toughest, most widespread California ceanothus, an evergreen, nitrogen-fixing chaparral shrub smothered in white bloom in spring. Drought-proof and alive with pollinators.

Fendler's ceanothus is a low, somewhat spiny nitrogen-fixer of Southwestern pine forests, with white flower clusters and important wildlife browse. Spreads modestly and fixes nitrogen on poor mountain soils.
Point Reyes ceanothus is a low, spreading evergreen groundcover ceanothus of the California coast, blanketed in blue bloom and tolerant of wind and salt. A premier nitrogen-fixing bank cover.
Desert ceanothus is a stiff, gray-green evergreen ceanothus of Southwestern high desert and chaparral, covered in pale blue-white bloom in spring. Exceptionally drought- and heat-tolerant.
Jersey tea, or prairie redroot, is a compact nitrogen-fixing prairie shrub with frothy white flower clusters that swarm with pollinators. Deep-rooted and drought-proof for meadows and dry borders.
Santa Barbara ceanothus is a compact evergreen California lilac with crinkled dark leaves and intense deep-blue spring bloom. One of the showiest nitrogen-fixing ceanothus for dry, sunny gardens.
Deerbrush is an open, deciduous ceanothus of western foothills with fragrant white-to-pale-blue plumes, named for the deer that browse it heavily. A fast nitrogen-fixing pioneer after fire.
