Chaparral whitethorn is a tall, spiny, blue-gray evergreen ceanothus that forms impenetrable nitrogen-fixing thickets on dry California slopes. Pale blue bloom draws clouds of pollinators.
Maritime ceanothus is a low, mounding evergreen groundcover from windswept California sea bluffs, with gray-green leaves and pale-to-deep blue bloom. Tough, salt-tolerant, and nitrogen-fixing.

Bigpod ceanothus is a large evergreen chaparral shrub of southern California, among the earliest to bloom, clouding hillsides white in late winter. A drought-proof nitrogen-fixer and pollinator resource.

Baker's manzanita is a rare California manzanita with smooth mahogany bark and pink urn-shaped flowers, prized in gardens for its sculptural form. Drought-tolerant and deer-resistant once established.
Hoary manzanita is a gray-hairy evergreen manzanita of western chaparral and conifer forest, with the genus's signature red bark and early bell flowers. A tough, deer-resistant pollinator shrub.
Hairy manzanita is a large Pacific Northwest manzanita with crooked red branches and white-pink spring bell flowers, followed by reddish berries for wildlife. Drought-hardy and deer-resistant.
Vine Hill manzanita is a rare, garden-beloved California manzanita with glossy leaves, smooth red bark, and abundant pink bloom. A choice deer-resistant evergreen for dry, well-drained sites.
Little Sur manzanita is a low, spreading groundcover manzanita from the Big Sur coast, hugging slopes with small bright leaves and early pink bloom. A first-rate deer-resistant evergreen groundcover.
Hooker's manzanita is a low, mounding California manzanita with glossy green leaves and shining red bark, useful as a refined groundcover or low shrub. Drought- and deer-resistant.
Pinemat manzanita is a flat, ground-hugging mat of an evergreen shrub from western mountain forests, rooting as it creeps over rock and sand. A hardy, deer-resistant alpine groundcover.
Pajaro manzanita is a striking large manzanita with blue-green leaves that flush bronze, smooth red bark, and very early pink bloom. A bold, drought- and deer-resistant evergreen specimen.

Greenleaf manzanita is a rounded mountain manzanita with bright apple-green leaves, mahogany bark, and pink urn flowers, widespread in western conifer forests. Drought-hardy, deer-resistant, and wildlife-rich.
Pringle manzanita is a tall, glandular-hairy manzanita of Southwestern mountains and high desert, with red bark and pink bloom. A heat- and drought-proof, deer-resistant evergreen for arid gardens.
Red barberry is a spiny, holly-leaved evergreen of the Southwestern desert, with fragrant yellow spring flowers and translucent red berries used for jelly. Drought-proof and deer-resistant.
Nevin's barberry is a rare, blue-gray evergreen California barberry with yellow flower clusters and red berries for birds. An endangered species in the wild, garden plantings aid its conservation.
California barberry is a glossy, holly-like evergreen with wavy spined leaflets, bright yellow late-winter flowers, and powder-blue berries. A versatile deer-resistant shrub for sun or part shade.
Texas barberry is a compact evergreen barberry of the Edwards Plateau, with spiny blue-green leaflets, yellow bloom, and red-to-yellow berries good for jelly. Heat-, drought-, and deer-resistant.

Canyon grape is a vigorous Southwestern grapevine that drapes desert canyons and washes, bearing small sweet-tart grapes devoured by birds and mammals. Heat- and drought-hardy once established; give it support and room.
Carolina holly is a deciduous holly of dry Southeastern woods, dropping its leaves to reveal red berries that feed birds into winter. As with all hollies, a male plant nearby is needed for fruit.
Sarvis holly is an uncommon deciduous holly of Southeastern swamps and pond edges, with downy leaves and dull red berries for wildlife. A conservation-worthy wetland shrub for acidic, wet ground.

Dahoon holly is a graceful evergreen holly of Southeastern wetlands, with narrow glossy leaves and heavy crops of red berries that light up winter and feed many birds. Excellent for wet, acidic sites.

Large gallberry, or sweet gallberry, is an evergreen wetland holly of the Southeast whose flowers yield prized gallberry honey and whose black fruit feeds birds. A suckering shrub for boggy, acidic ground.
Possumhaw is a deciduous holly grown for its spectacular winter display of scarlet berries clustered along bare gray branches, stripped slowly by birds. Adaptable to wet or dry soils in sun.
Inkberry is a tidy native evergreen holly with black berries, widely used as a well-behaved boxwood alternative for hedges and foundations. Suckers into colonies; thrives on moist, acidic soil.
Myrtle holly is a fine-textured evergreen holly of Southeastern wetlands, with tiny needle-like leaves and red berries. A drought- and flood-tolerant small tree once established, valuable for birds.
White honeysuckle is a sprawling, twining native honeysuckle of Texas and the Southwest, with creamy spring flowers and translucent red berries for birds. Drought-tough for dry slopes and fences.
Orange honeysuckle is a twining Pacific Northwest vine hung with whorls of orange-red trumpet flowers that hummingbirds chase, followed by red berries. A well-behaved native climber for part shade.

Sevenleaf creeper is a Texas-endemic woodbine relative with handsome seven-fingered leaves that turn red in fall and dark berries relished by birds. A heat- and drought-hardy native climber for walls and arbors.
Desert snowberry is a wiry Western shrub bearing fragrant, tubular pink flowers that draw hummingbirds, followed by waxy white berries for birds. Drought- and cold-hardy for dry, rocky slopes.

Creeping snowberry is a low, trailing Western groundcover shrub with small pink flowers and white berries, rooting as it spreads over dry, shaded slopes. A useful native bank cover under oaks and pines.

Pyramid magnolia is a small deciduous magnolia of rich Southeastern woods, with big paddle-shaped leaves splayed in tiers and creamy white spring flowers. An elegant understory tree for moist, acidic shade.

Lanceleaf greenbrier, or Jackson vine, is a nearly thornless evergreen Southern vine long cut for holiday greenery, with glossy leaves and dark berries for birds. A handsome native climber for fences and arbors.

Swamp bay is an aromatic evergreen tree of Southeastern wetlands, a key larval host for palamedes and spicebush swallowtails, with blue fruit for birds. Note that laurel wilt disease now threatens redbays across the South.

Slimleaf pawpaw is a sandhill pawpaw of the Southeast with large, fragrant white flowers and edible yellow fruit, and a host for the zebra swallowtail. Drought-hardy and deer-resistant on deep, dry sand.

Bigflower pawpaw is a Florida-scrub shrub bearing showy, fragrant white flowers up to four inches across and sweet edible fruit, hosting the zebra swallowtail. A drought- and deer-resistant scrub specialist.

Smallflower pawpaw is a small understory pawpaw of Southeastern woods with maroon bell flowers and edible fruit, a zebra swallowtail host. Spreads modestly in dry to moist acidic shade and resists deer.

Dwarf pawpaw is a knee-high pawpaw of Florida pine flatwoods with creamy-to-maroon flowers and edible fruit, hosting the zebra swallowtail. A diminutive, deer-resistant groundlayer shrub for dry sand.

Eastern wahoo is a native shrub or small tree grown for its dramatic fall show of rose-red capsules that split to reveal scarlet seeds, devoured by birds. Note the seeds are toxic to people. Best in moist part shade.

Carolina buckthorn is a well-behaved native buckthorn (unlike the invasive European species), a small tree with glossy leaves and berries that ripen red to black for birds. A handsome wildlife shrub for sun or part shade.
Redberry buckthorn is a stiff, spiny California evergreen shrub with tiny holly-like leaves and bright red berries that feed birds. Exceptionally drought- and heat-tolerant for dry chaparral gardens.
Lanceleaf buckthorn is a modest native buckthorn of Eastern and Midwestern woods and glades, with inconspicuous flowers and black fruit for birds. A larval host and understory shrub for varied soils.

Antelope horns is a tough, low milkweed of dry plains and rocky ground, its greenish star-flowers in domed clusters and curved horn-like pods. A drought-proof monarch host for the South and Southwest.
Tall milkweed is a slender western milkweed of foothill woodland edges and canyons, with white flower clusters. An important monarch and pollinator host across California's interior ranges.
Poke milkweed is one of the few milkweeds for shade, with drooping clusters of white-and-lavender flowers in open woods. A monarch host that brings milkweed value to dappled, moist gardens.
Fewflower milkweed flags wet meadows and marsh edges of the Southeast with vivid red-orange flowers atop tall slender stems. A striking monarch host and hummingbird draw for rain gardens.
Purple milkweed rivals the common milkweed but with deeper rose-purple blooms and a tidier, non-aggressive habit. A choice monarch host for sun to light shade in average garden soil.

Rush milkweed is a nearly leafless desert milkweed of green pencil-like stems, blooming through much of the year. An essential monarch host and nectar source in the hottest, driest Southwestern gardens.
Green comet milkweed bears rounded clusters of subtle green flowers on prairies, glades, and dry barrens. A drought-hardy, well-behaved monarch host for lean soils.
Mouse-ear tickseed is a low, stoloniferous coreopsis for the front of part-shade borders, with golden daisies above eared leaves. Spreads gently into a cheerful groundcover.
Leafstem tickseed is a showy California annual that carpets open ground with large golden flowers after wet winters. A quick, drought-adapted splash for wildflower meadows.
Douglas's tickseed is a diminutive California annual that paints spring slopes and flats with bright yellow blooms. A tiny but vivid native for sunny, well-drained sites.

Giant coreopsis is the dramatic, thick-trunked tickseed of the California coast, summer-dormant then crowned with golden daisies. A sculptural, salt- and drought-tolerant subshrub for seaside gardens.
Leavenworth's tickseed is Florida's state wildflower, blooming nearly year-round with golden, dark-centered daisies. A self-sowing annual that fills roadsides and wet meadows with color.
Sea dahlia is a fleshy-stemmed coastal coreopsis of southern California bluffs, with large yellow daisies in spring. Drought- and salt-tolerant, summer-dormant, and showy in seaside gardens.
Georgia tickseed is the rare pink-flowered coreopsis of Southeastern bogs and wet pine savannas, with rush-like leaves. A standout for the moist edges of rain gardens.
Stiff tickseed is a tough, rhizomatous prairie coreopsis with stiff three-lobed leaves and clean yellow daisies. Drought-proof and colony-forming for meadows and tough sites.
Pink tickseed is a delicate, threadleaf coreopsis with rose-pink, yellow-centered flowers, spreading into airy mats on moist sandy soil. A long-blooming pollinator favorite for damp meadows.
Sanguine coneflower is a slender Southern coneflower of open pine woods and sandy prairies, with drooping rose-pink rays. A drought-hardy coneflower whose seed heads feed finches.

Marsh rattlesnake-master is a wetland eryngo of the Southeast, topped with steely blue, thistle-like flower heads that swarm with bees and wasps. Architectural structure for rain gardens and pond edges.
Leavenworth's eryngo is a spectacular violet-purple annual eryngo that turns late-summer prairies amethyst, a magnet for pollinators. Thrives on hot, dry, alkaline ground.

Rattlesnake master is an iconic prairie plant with yucca-like blue-green leaves and globe-shaped white flower heads alive with specialist bees and wasps. Bold structure and a deep taproot make it drought-proof.
Arizona blanketflower brightens the spring desert with clear yellow daisies on a compact, drought-hardy plant. A cheerful pollinator annual for hot, dry, low-water gardens.
Red dome blanketflower is a long-blooming Southwestern native with yellow rays around a domed red center, shrugging off heat and drought. A durable, deer-resistant pollinator plant for xeric beds.
Pincushion daisy lifts fragrant, ball-shaped flower heads on slender bare stalks above a basal rosette, dancing over dry prairies. A drought- and deer-resistant pollinator charmer.
Cylindrical blazing star is a compact, drought-tough liatris of dry prairies and glades, with button-like rose-purple heads. A late-summer nectar source ideal for lean, rocky soils.
Prairie blazing star sends up tall, dense wands of rose-purple bloom that open top-down, thronged by butterflies and bees. A signature tallgrass-prairie plant and superb cut flower.
Shortleaf blazing star is a fine-textured Southeastern liatris of deep sands, with airy spikes of late-season purple bloom over grassy foliage. Excellent for sandy pollinator gardens.
Eastern bee balm is the earliest, most compact native bee balm, with lavender-pink, dark-speckled flowers and good mildew resistance. A tidy, drought-tolerant pollinator plant for sun or part shade.
Lemon bee balm is a fragrant annual with tiered whorls of pink-lavender flowers, beloved by bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds. Self-sows readily and makes a citrus-scented herbal tea.

Scarlet bee balm blazes with shaggy red flowers that hummingbirds and butterflies cannot resist, spreading into colonies in moist, rich soil. The classic native bee balm; give it air flow to limit mildew.
California coneflower is a stately Sierra wet-meadow native with tall stems and towering cone-centered yellow daisies. A bold pollinator plant for moist, sunny mountain gardens.
Rough coneflower carries large golden daisies with tall, thimble-like brown cones over coarse foliage, thriving on dry prairies and glades. Drought-hardy with seed heads that feed finches.
Sweet coneflower is a tall, anise-scented coneflower covered in masses of small golden daisies through late summer, a pollinator powerhouse. Tolerates clay and wet feet; finches strip its seed heads.
Engelmann's sage is a compact Texas native with soft blue-violet flower spikes over gray-green foliage, thriving on dry limestone. A drought- and deer-resistant pollinator plant for hot sites.
Mountain sage is a fall-blooming shrub of West Texas canyons, lighting up with brilliant scarlet tubular flowers just as hummingbirds migrate south. Drought-hardy and deer-resistant for part shade.
Cedar sage is a shade-loving Texas Hill Country sage with scarlet flowers that draw hummingbirds, happiest in the dappled light beneath junipers and oaks. Drought- and deer-resistant for dry shade.
Creeping sage is a low, aromatic California groundcover sage that mats over dry slopes with gray foliage and blue-violet flower spikes. Evergreen, drought- and deer-resistant cover for sunny banks.

Prostrate vervain is a tough, ground-hugging pioneer that threads small blue-purple flowers across hot, disturbed, and compacted ground. Modest but a reliable long-season nectar source for small bees.

Blue vervain sends up candelabra spikes of violet-blue flowers over moist meadows and stream edges, blooming for months and feeding many bees and butterflies. A vertical accent for rain gardens; seeds feed birds.
Shortspike bluestem is a clumping Southeastern warm-season grass with slender stems and fluffy seed heads, glowing copper in fall. A host for skipper butterflies and cover for ground-nesting birds.
Chalky bluestem is a striking grass of the Southeastern coastal plain with waxy blue-white stems and silvery autumn seed heads. Drought-tough on sandy soils and a larval host for skippers.
Broomsedge bluestem turns old fields and roadsides warm orange-tan through winter, a pioneer of poor, disturbed ground. Provides cover and seed for wildlife and hosts several skippers.
Black grama is a fine, wiry desert grassland grass that spreads by arching stolons, a backbone of Southwestern rangeland. Exceptionally drought-tolerant forage and cover.
Chino grama is a tufted grama of rocky desert slopes with the genus's distinctive flag-like seed spikes. A tough, drought-proof bunchgrass for hot, lean, limestone soils.
Eastern narrowleaf sedge is a clumping woodland sedge with arching blue-green blades, excellent as a lawn alternative or massed groundcover in moist shade.
Yellowfruit sedge is an adaptable clumping sedge of wet meadows and disturbed moist ground, with knobby yellow-green seed heads. A quick colonizer for rain gardens.
Water sedge is a rhizomatous sedge of marshes, fens, and pond margins across the North, forming lush stands in standing water. Key wetland habitat and waterfowl food.
Bebb's sedge is a tufted wetland sedge with dense oval seed heads, common in sunny marshes and wet meadows. Its seeds feed waterfowl and songbirds.
Plains oval sedge is a drought-tolerant clumping sedge of prairies and open ground, with round, beadlike seed heads. Tougher and sunnier than most sedges.
Brome-like sedge forms soft, arching tussocks of fine bright-green foliage in wet woods and seeps, a graceful groundcover for moist shade.
Oval-leaf sedge is a small, adaptable woodland sedge that tolerates dry shade, with tidy clumps and capitate seed heads. A fine native lawn or groundcover alternative.
Bristly sedge is a bold wetland sedge with shaggy, bristly seed heads dangling over pond and marsh edges. Striking structure and cover for aquatic wildlife.
Fringed sedge hangs long, fuzzy, drooping seed catkins over wet meadows and stream banks, a graceful accent for rain gardens and pond edges.
Crested sedge is a clumping wet-meadow sedge with bristly, star-like seed heads, quick to establish in sunny moist ground. Good for wetland restoration and rain gardens.
Frank's sedge bears bottlebrush-like cylindrical seed heads in summer on tolerant clumps that thrive in wet, disturbed ground. An easy sedge for damp sites.
Graceful sedge drapes thread-fine, nodding seed spikes over arching clumps in moist woodlands, one of the most elegant native sedges for shade gardens.
Hairy green sedge is a soft, fuzzy-leaved clumping sedge tolerant of dry shade and clay, useful as a durable groundcover under trees.
Lakebank sedge is a tall, rhizomatous sedge that armors shorelines and marshes with blue-green foliage, excellent for erosion control on wet banks.
Largehead sedge is a robust Pacific-coast dune sedge with spiny, oversized seed heads, spreading by stout rhizomes to bind beach sand.
Nebraska sedge is a vigorous, rhizomatous western wetland sedge, a workhorse of wet-meadow and streambank restoration and important forage and cover.
Knotsheath sedge is a wetland sedge with reflexed, spreading seed heads, thriving in marshes, swamps, and wet ditches across the North.
Beaked sedge is a blue-green, rhizomatous sedge of fens, bogs, and shallow water, forming extensive northern wetland stands prized by waterfowl.
Sartwell's sedge spreads by rhizomes into uniform stands in wet prairies and marshes, useful for stabilizing and restoring sunny wetlands.
Low woodland sedge spreads gently into soft colonies in moist bottomland woods, an easy native groundcover for damp shade.
Awlfruit sedge forms big soft tussocks with knobby greenish seed heads in wet woods, ditches, and marsh edges, quick to establish for rain gardens.
California oatgrass is a cool-season bunchgrass of western meadows and oak woodlands, a key native lawn and meadow grass that greens up with winter rains.
Timber oatgrass is a compact cool-season bunchgrass of mountain meadows and openings, hardy and drought-tolerant at high elevations.
Poverty oatgrass is a tough little bunchgrass that thrives on the driest, poorest, most acidic soils where little else grows, with curly basal leaves.
Onespike oatgrass is a tiny western bunchgrass of dry, rocky openings and scablands, drought-hardy and well suited to lean rock-garden conditions.
Tapered rosette grass is a low, soft-haired native bunchgrass that forms winter rosettes and flowers in two flushes, a larval host for skippers in open woods and fields.
Variable panicgrass is a broad-leaved woodland rosette grass that stays semi-evergreen, providing cover and seed in dappled shade and hosting skipper larvae.
Plains lovegrass is a clumping warm-season grass topped with airy, shimmering purple-tinged seed clouds, drought-tough and beautiful in masses.
Sand lovegrass is a graceful sandhill grass with billowing, reddish-purple flowering panicles that catch the light, ideal for dry, sandy gardens.
Mountain muhly is a fine-textured bunchgrass of western mountains and high plains, drought- and cold-hardy with delicate flowering stems.
Seep muhly forms tidy mounds of fine foliage topped each fall with a haze of rosy-pink flowering clouds, a tough, showy choice for dry limestone soils.
Purple muhly sends up slender, deep purple flowering spikes above fine foliage in fall, a striking, drought-proof accent grass for the Southwest.
Bitter panicgrass is a blue-gray, rhizomatous dune grass of the Atlantic and Gulf coasts, salt- and drought-tolerant and excellent for stabilizing sand.
Witchgrass is an annual native grass whose huge, lacy seed panicles break off and tumble in the wind. Quick cover for disturbed ground; abundant seed for birds.
Vine mesquite is a stoloniferous Southwestern grass that creeps over swales and washes, knitting soil together for erosion control on tough, intermittently wet ground.
Redtop panicgrass is a clumping wetland grass with reddish, airy seed heads, thriving at pond edges and in wet meadows. Good structure and seed for rain gardens.
Mesa dropseed is a wiry desert bunchgrass with open, zigzagging seed panicles, exceptionally drought-tolerant forage and cover for the arid Southwest.
Big sacaton is a large, fountain-like bunchgrass of Southwestern swales and grasslands, a bold specimen that provides cover and binds soil on heavy or alkaline ground.
Rock penstemon is a tough, somewhat woody Texas native that wedges into limestone crevices and blooms cherry-red for months in summer heat. A hummingbird favorite for hot, dry, alkaline rock gardens.
Buckley's penstemon is an early-blooming Plains beardtongue with waxy blue-green leaves and soft pink-lavender spikes, thriving on dry sand and caliche.
Eastern gray beardtongue is a soft-haired Appalachian penstemon with lavender-purple tubes, happy in the part shade and average soils of woodland edges.
Cleveland's beardtongue lights up the southern California desert in spring with magenta-pink tubes on wiry stems, a hummingbird magnet for hot, rocky slopes.
Davidson's penstemon is a ground-hugging alpine mat smothered in violet-blue trumpets, a jewel for rock gardens and high, sharply drained sites.
Hot-rock penstemon bears cream-white tubes with fine purple lines on dry, rocky western slopes, a drought-proof beardtongue for lean, gravelly soil.
Shrubby penstemon is a low, woody-based evergreen penstemon of the Northwest, covered in large lavender-blue flowers, superb in rock gardens and dry borders.
Eastern smooth beardtongue carries airy spikes of white, faintly violet-tinged tubes over glossy foliage, an adaptable native for sun to part shade in average soil.
Manyflower beardtongue is a tall, airy white-flowered penstemon of Florida and Southeastern sandhills, drought-hardy and excellent for sandy pollinator gardens.
Cupleaf beardtongue is a striking Southern penstemon with scarlet tubes and round, clasping blue-gray leaves encircling the stem, irresistible to hummingbirds.
Beaked penstemon blooms long into summer with slender orange-red tubes on a bushy plant, a hummingbird favorite for dry Southwestern mountain gardens.
Small's beardtongue is a showy Appalachian penstemon with dense spikes of rose-purple, white-throated tubes, thriving on rocky slopes in sun to part shade.
Royal penstemon is a Great Basin showstopper with brilliant azure-blue trumpets on stout stems, demanding only sun and very sharp drainage.
Showy penstemon lives up to its name with tall wands of large blue-violet flowers, a vigorous, drought-tolerant beardtongue for southern California gardens.
Gulf Coast penstemon is unusual among beardtongues for tolerating wet, heavy soils, offering lavender-purple bloom for rain gardens and damp borders in the South.
Thurber's penstemon forms a wiry, rounded subshrub dotted with pink-lavender tubes, exceptionally drought- and heat-tolerant for Southwestern xeric gardens.
Hill Country penstemon is a Texas endemic with vivid rose-pink, candy-striped tubes on limestone hills, a heat- and drought-proof beardtongue for alkaline soils.
Wright's penstemon opens early with rose-red tubes on blue-gray foliage, a drought-hardy Southwestern beardtongue beloved by hummingbirds.
Yellow bush lupine is a fast, fragrant coastal shrub lupine that mounds into a cloud of butter-yellow spikes, fixing nitrogen and stabilizing dunes.
Miniature lupine is a cheerful little annual that scatters blue-and-white spikes across western grasslands and roadsides each spring. Reseeds freely in sunny meadows.
Tailcup lupine is a silvery-leaved perennial lupine of the dry Intermountain West, with violet-blue spikes that feed bees and host blue butterflies.
Whitewhorl lupine is a California annual with tiered whorls of creamy white-to-yellow flowers, a quick spring filler for sunny, well-drained ground.
Sierra lupine is a low, woolly perennial of California's mountains, with purple-pink spikes over silvery foliage, thriving on dry, rocky slopes.
Big Bend bluebonnet is the towering Texas bluebonnet of the Chihuahuan Desert, sending up dramatic deep-blue spikes up to four feet tall after winter rains.
Sky lupine carpets California hillsides in spring with fragrant blue-and-white spikes, a classic wildflower-meadow annual that reseeds readily.
Wild lupine is the sole larval host of the endangered Karner blue butterfly, sending blue-violet spikes over sandy oak savannas and pine barrens. A keystone of sandy-soil restoration.
Silky lupine is a widespread western perennial with silky-haired leaves and blue-purple spikes, a nitrogen-fixing pollinator and butterfly-host plant for dry meadows.
Texas bluebonnet is the beloved state flower, blanketing spring roadsides in deep blue with white-tipped spikes. A reliable, self-sowing annual for sunny, well-drained ground.
Valley lupine is a slender California annual lupine of grassy valleys and foothills, adding blue-violet spring spikes to wildflower meadows.
Yellow-and-white monkeyflower is a charming California annual of vernal seeps and wet flats, with two-toned blooms that follow the spring moisture.
Bigelow's monkeyflower is a tiny desert annual that paints rocky washes magenta-pink after winter rains, a fleeting but vivid Mojave wildflower.
Kellogg's monkeyflower is a small California annual whose deep magenta-purple flowers light up open slopes and burns in spring.
Lewis's monkeyflower is a showy perennial of western mountain streamsides, with large rose-pink snapdragon-like flowers that draw hummingbirds. Loves cool, wet ground.
Primrose monkeyflower is a tiny, mat-forming perennial of wet montane meadows, dotting the ground with bright yellow flowers over creeping rosettes.
Southern bush monkeyflower is a sticky-leaved California subshrub covered in apricot-to-buff trumpets through spring, drought-tolerant and beloved by hummingbirds.
Red bush monkeyflower is a coastal southern California subshrub with brick-red to orange tubular flowers, a drought-proof, hummingbird-magnet for sunny dry banks.
Western dog violet is a low blue-violet violet of meadows and open woods, and a key larval host for several fritillary butterflies across the West.
Sweet white violet is a dainty, fragrant white violet that carpets cool, moist eastern woodlands, spreading by runners into a delicate groundcover.
Canadian white violet is a taller woodland violet with white, yellow-eyed flowers flushed purple on the back, blooming over a long season in rich shade.
Marsh blue violet thrives in wet meadows, seeps, and stream edges, with blue-violet flowers held above the leaves. A fritillary host for damp, sunny-to-shaded ground.
Stream violet brightens moist Pacific woodlands with cheerful yellow flowers in early spring, spreading into soft colonies along shaded creeks.
Lance-leaved violet is a bog and wet-meadow violet with narrow lance-shaped leaves and small white flowers, a fritillary host for sunny, saturated ground.
Small white violet is a tiny creeping violet of wet montane meadows and seeps, dotting the ground with white blooms beside snowmelt streams.
Nuttall's violet is a sun-loving prairie violet with golden flowers, drought-tolerant on the dry grasslands and foothills of the West. A fritillary host.
Bird's-foot violet is the showiest native violet, with large pansy-like blue-violet flowers over finely divided leaves on dry, sandy, acidic barrens. A fritillary host that resents rich or wet soil.
Downy yellow violet is an upright woodland violet with soft-haired stems and clear yellow flowers, a cheerful spring bloomer for dry to moist shade.
Arrowleaf violet is a sun-tolerant violet of open woods, fields, and barrens, with arrow-shaped leaves and rich violet flowers. A larval host for fritillaries.
Common blue violet is the tough, adaptable violet of lawns and woodland edges across the East, with blue-violet (sometimes white) flowers and edible blooms. A workhorse fritillary host.
Valley violet is a small yellow-flowered violet of western grasslands and sagebrush valleys, blooming early before summer drought. A fritillary host.
Longflower alumroot is a graceful Southeastern woodland heuchera with airy wands of tubular pink-white flowers above mottled leaves, ideal for shaded rock gardens.
Island alumroot is a robust Channel Islands heuchera forming big mounds of lobed leaves topped with foamy white-pink sprays, a tough evergreen for dry California shade.
Littleleaf alumroot is a compact Rocky Mountain heuchera of rocky slopes and cliffs, with greenish-white flower spikes, hardy and drought-tolerant at elevation.
Seaside alumroot is a fuzzy-leaved California coastal heuchera with pink-tinged flower plumes, tolerant of wind, salt, and dry shade.
Prairie alumroot is the sun- and drought-tolerant heuchera of the tallgrass prairie, with greenish bell flowers and deep roots, thriving where most heucheras would fail.
Pink alumroot is a delicate heuchera of western rock crevices and cliffs, with rosy-pink flowers on wiry stems, a gem for sharply drained rock gardens.
Coral bells is the classic red-flowered heuchera of Southwestern mountains, ringing with scarlet bells that hummingbirds chase. The parent of countless garden hybrids.
Hairy alumroot is a heat- and humidity-tough Appalachian heuchera that blooms late with frothy white-pink sprays over big maple-like leaves, excellent for Southern shade.
Golden columbine is a long-spurred, fragrant yellow columbine of Southwestern canyons and seeps, blooming for months and drawing hummingbirds and hawkmoths.
Serpentine columbine is a tall California columbine of seeps and serpentine slopes, with nodding red-and-yellow flowers that hummingbirds adore.
Yellow columbine is a soft-yellow, nodding-flowered columbine of western mountain meadows and forest openings, hardy and charming for cool gardens.
Jones's columbine is a tiny alpine treasure of high limestone scree, with outsized violet-blue flowers over blue-gray tufts. A coveted, demanding rock-garden plant.
Thimbleweed is a prairie anemone with greenish-white flowers that mature into cylindrical, thimble-like seed heads bursting into woolly cotton. Drought-hardy and deer-resistant.
Pacific anemone is a tufted western anemone of rocky meadows and slopes, with cream-to-reddish flowers and silky seed heads, tough and deer-resistant.
Desert anemone is an early-spring Southwestern anemone that rises from tubers after winter rains with white-to-pink flowers, then retreats by summer. Deer-resistant.
Padre's shooting star is an early California native whose swept-back magenta-pink petals form little 'shooting stars' over basal rosettes, going dormant by summer.
Henderson's shooting star brightens spring oak woodlands of the West with rose-purple, dart-like flowers, then dies back as the dry season arrives.
Humboldt lily is a statuesque California lily with whorled leaves and nodding orange, maroon-spotted, turk's-cap flowers, lighting up dry woodland edges in summer.
Leopard lily is a spectacular western streamside lily, towering with dozens of orange-red, spotted turk's-cap flowers that hummingbirds and swallowtails visit. Loves wet feet.
Wood lily is a fiery upward-facing orange-red lily of prairies, savannas, and open woods, increasingly scarce in the wild. A stunning but deer-favored native.
Pink fawn lily is a choice Pacific Northwest spring ephemeral with nodding rose-pink flowers over mottled leaves, carpeting moist woods and stream banks before fading by summer.
Richardson's geranium is a western mountain cranesbill with white-to-pale-pink, lavender-veined flowers over deeply cut leaves, a long-blooming, deer-resistant native for moist shade.
Early meadow-rue is a delicate woodland plant whose dangling, tassel-like flowers and lacy columbine-like foliage catch the light in spring. Deer-resistant and airy for shade.
Western foamflower spreads into soft mats of maple-like leaves topped with frothy spires of tiny white flowers, a graceful native groundcover for moist Pacific shade.
Yellow trillium is a Southeastern woodland trillium with lemon-scented, upright yellow flowers over beautifully mottled leaves, a prized but slow-growing spring gem for rich shade.
Painted trillium is an exquisite acid-loving woodland trillium with white petals brushed crimson at the base, demanding the cool, moist, humus-rich soil of conifer and mountain forests.
Franciscan bluebells hang clusters of sky-blue, pink-budded trumpets over lush foliage in moist Southwestern mountain meadows and streamsides, a long-blooming pollinator favorite.
Western aster is a rhizomatous aster of meadows and sagebrush flats across the Intermountain West, with lavender-blue rays for late-season pollinators.
Pacific aster is the common coastal aster of California, blooming for months with violet-blue daisies, tolerant of wind, salt, and dry summers.
Bushy aster is a much-branched, airy aster studded with small pale blue-to-white daisies in fall, an adaptable late-nectar source for sunny borders and meadows.
Leafy aster is a violet-flowered aster of western mountain meadows and streamsides, its leafy bracts cupping the flower heads, blooming through the cool high-country summer.
Calico aster sprays horizontal branches of tiny white daisies with pink-to-purple centers that change color with age, a fine-textured fall nectar plant for sun or part shade.
Bigleaf aster carpets dry woodland floors with large heart-shaped leaves, sending up pale lavender daisies in late summer. A tough groundcover and nectar source for shade.
Hyssopleaf boneset is a fine-leaved, drought-tough boneset that foams with flat clusters of tiny white flowers in fall, a magnet for small pollinators on dry, sandy ground.
Roundleaf boneset bears flat-topped clusters of fuzzy white flowers over rounded, clasping leaves, an adaptable Southeastern native for sunny to lightly shaded pollinator gardens.

White snakeroot lights up the autumn shade with frothy white flower clusters, one of the best late-season nectar plants for woodland gardens. Note: foliage is toxic to livestock (the historic cause of milk sickness).
White mistflower is a fall-blooming Texas shrub that erupts in fragrant, fuzzy white-to-pink flower clouds just as migrating butterflies and pollinators pass through. Drought- and deer-resistant.
Hammock snakeroot is a Florida native bearing airy white flower clusters in late fall, a key nectar source for butterflies in dry hammocks and pinelands.
Slender goldentop is a fine-leaved, flat-topped goldenrod relative of sandy coastal-plain soils, covered in tiny yellow flowers that swarm with fall pollinators.
Grass-leaved goldenrod spreads into colonies of narrow leaves topped with flat clusters of golden flowers, a robust late-season nectar plant for sunny meadows and damp ground.
Prairie goldenrod (upland white goldenrod) is an unusual white-flowered, flat-topped goldenrod of dry prairies and glades, drought-hardy and excellent for lean, alkaline soils.
Ohio goldenrod is a flat-topped goldenrod of wet prairies, fens, and lakeshores, its golden heads feeding pollinators on calcareous, moist ground.
Wholeleaf rosinweed is a sturdy prairie sunflower-relative with bright yellow daisies on rough, unbranched stems, a long-lived nectar plant whose seeds feed finches.
Compass plant is a tallgrass-prairie icon with huge, deeply cut leaves that align north-south and a towering spire of yellow flowers over a taproot many feet deep. Goldfinches strip its seeds.
White doll's daisy is a billowing, aster-like native smothered in small white-to-pink daisies in fall, thriving in moist meadows and rain gardens and feeding late pollinators.
Zigzag iris is a Louisiana iris with rich blue-violet flowers on distinctively bent stems, thriving in wet, rich soils and at pond edges in sun to part shade.
Dixie iris is a Southeastern Louisiana iris of swamps and wet ditches, with large blue-purple flowers, perfect for boggy, sunny spots and water-garden margins.
Del Norte iris is a dainty Pacific Coast iris with golden-to-lavender flowers over grassy clumps, ideal for woodland edges and rock gardens with summer-dry shade.
Slender blue flag is a graceful, narrow-leaved iris of coastal marshes and wet meadows, with delicate blue-violet flowers on slim stems, fine for bog and rain gardens.
Shasta iris is a northern California woodland iris with pale cream-and-veined flowers over slender clumps, suited to dry shade beneath oaks and pines.
Wild garlic is a widespread native onion with nodding pink-white flower clusters and edible bulbs and leaves, an easy, deer-resistant bulb for meadows and woodland edges.
Largeflower onion is a small desert onion that opens star-like pink flowers after spring rains in the Southwest, drought-hardy and edible.
One-leaf onion is a California native onion with airy umbels of clear pink flowers, easy and showy in borders, with edible bulbs and foliage.
Swamp onion is a tall western onion of wet mountain meadows, with dense rose-purple flower heads beloved by bees, and edible throughout.
Giant spiderwort is a robust Texas spiderwort with large violet-blue, three-petaled flowers that open each morning over arching grassy leaves, tough and long-blooming in spring.
Hairystem spiderwort is a compact Southeastern spiderwort of rocky woods and glades, with violet-blue flowers on fuzzy stems, well suited to dry, sunny banks.
Stalked spiderwort is a slender central-states spiderwort with blue-purple to pinkish flowers over grassy foliage, an easy, drought-tolerant native for sunny meadows.
Zigzag spiderwort is a woodland spiderwort with zigzagging stems and deep blue-violet flowers, blooming well into summer in the moist shade where other spiderworts fade.
Prairie ironweed crowns wet prairies and swales with flat clusters of intense red-purple flowers, a bold, deer-resistant late-summer nectar plant for moist, sunny ground.
Woolly ironweed is a compact, silvery-leaved Texas ironweed with magenta-purple flowers, exceptionally drought- and heat-tolerant for dry, alkaline pollinator gardens.
New York ironweed towers in moist meadows and stream edges with broad clusters of rich red-purple flowers atop tall stems, a striking, deer-resistant magnet for butterflies.
White blue-eyed grass is a tufted iris-relative with pale white-to-blue starry flowers over grassy fans, a charming little native for sunny, dry-to-moist meadows.
Narrowleaf blue-eyed grass dots moist meadows and woodland edges with blue-violet, yellow-eyed flowers over grassy clumps, an easy and cheerful native groundlayer.
Eastern blue-eyed grass is a slender coastal-plain species with pale blue flowers, thriving in moist to wet, sandy, acidic soils of meadows and savannas.
Golden-eyed grass is a Pacific-coast blue-eyed-grass relative with bright yellow flowers, happy in wet meadows, seeps, and the moist edges of bog and rain gardens.
Prairie blue-eyed grass brings pale blue-to-white starry flowers to dry prairies and glades, a drought-tolerant little native for sunny, lean soils.
Whitestem evening primrose opens large white flowers at dusk that fade pink by morning, a night-bloomer of dry western plains pollinated by hawkmoths.
Common evening primrose is a tall biennial whose lemon-yellow flowers open at evening for moths; its seeds are a favorite winter food of goldfinches. A pioneer of open, disturbed ground.
Birdcage evening primrose is a desert annual whose big white moth-flowers fade pink, its dried stems curling into a cage that tumbles across the sand. A Mojave and Sonoran spring spectacle.
Hooker's evening primrose is a stout western native with tall spikes of large yellow evening flowers, thriving in moist meadows, ditches, and streamsides.
Slenderstalk beeblossom is an airy beeblossom (formerly Gaura) with wands of dancing white-to-pink flowers over wiry stems, a long-blooming, drought-tolerant native for sunny borders.
Yellow evening primrose is a low, stemless western native whose bright yellow flowers open from a basal rosette at evening, hardy on dry plains and foothills.
Nuttall's evening primrose is a slender white-flowered evening primrose of the northern Great Plains, opening at dusk for moths on dry, sandy ground.
Pale evening primrose spreads by rhizomes into drifts of fragrant white flowers that blush pink, anchoring dunes and sandy western flats. Tough and drought-proof.
Little sundrops is a dainty, day-blooming evening primrose with nodding buds and small yellow flowers, a well-behaved native for moist meadows and the front of borders.
Desert evening primrose is a low desert annual whose large yellow flowers open at dusk after winter rains, a fleeting Southwestern wildflower for hot, sandy sites.
Diamondpetal evening primrose sends up tall spikes of diamond-shaped yellow evening flowers on deep sands of the central plains, a biennial that lights up late-summer dusk.
Southern beeblossom is an airy, long-blooming beeblossom of Southeastern sandy soils, with slender wands of small white-to-pink flowers that float above the foliage.
Pink evening primrose smothers itself in cup-shaped pink flowers in spring and spreads enthusiastically by rhizome and seed. Drought-proof and showy — but give it room to roam.
Scarlet beeblossom is a long-blooming, drought-proof beeblossom with airy spikes of orange-red-to-pink flowers, a tough, deer-resistant pollinator plant for hot, dry gardens.
Spreading phlox forms low evergreen mats smothered in pink, white, or pale-blue flowers each spring, a tough cushion for western rock gardens and dry slopes.
Annual phlox (Drummond's phlox) is a Texas native that blankets spring fields in vivid pink, red, and purple, an easy self-sowing annual for sunny wildflower plantings.
Florida phlox carries clusters of rose-pink to lavender flowers atop slender stems in Southeastern pinelands, a long-blooming pollinator plant for sun to light shade.
Smooth phlox brings tall, mildew-resistant clusters of rose-pink to magenta flowers to moist meadows and prairie edges, a clean-foliaged native for sun to part shade.
Longleaf phlox dots dry western slopes and sagebrush flats with fragrant pink-to-white flowers in spring, a drought-hardy native for rock gardens and xeric beds.
Santa Fe phlox is a long-blooming Southwestern phlox with bright magenta-pink flowers over a low mound, exceptionally heat- and drought-tolerant for hot, rocky gardens.
Trailing phlox is a Southeastern evergreen mat phlox of sandy pinelands, carpeting the ground with pink-to-white flowers in early spring. A fine, drought-tolerant groundcover.
Showy phlox brightens Pacific Northwest woodlands and sagebrush slopes with notched pink-to-white flowers in spring, a charming native for sunny to lightly shaded dry sites.
Comfortroot is a Southeastern rosemallow with large creamy-yellow flowers centered in maroon, blooming in moist pine savannas and bog edges. A bold pollinator plant for damp, sunny ground.
Rock hibiscus is a wiry desert subshrub of the Southwest with delicate pale-pink, dark-eyed flowers off and on through the year, thriving on the driest, rockiest ground.
Swamp rosemallow is a towering wetland hibiscus with enormous, fragrant pale-pink flowers and big velvety leaves, a dramatic native for pond edges, marshes, and rain gardens.
Heartleaf rosemallow is a Texas and Southwestern subshrub that bears brilliant true-red flowers nearly year-round, a heat- and drought-proof magnet for hummingbirds.
Leafy Jacob's ladder is a long-blooming Rocky Mountain native with clusters of violet-blue, yellow-centered flowers over ferny ladder-like foliage, ideal for moist mountain gardens.
Western Jacob's ladder is a tall, moisture-loving native of wet western meadows and streamsides, with airy clusters of blue flowers above delicate divided leaves.
Showy Jacob's ladder is a compact alpine and montane native with bright blue, yellow-eyed flowers over tidy ladder-leaved tufts, a charmer for rock gardens.
Sky pilot is a high-alpine cushion plant of rocky scree and tundra, crowned with fragrant violet-blue flower clusters over sticky, skunky foliage. A prize for the alpine rock garden.
Narrowleaf obedient plant raises early spikes of pink-lavender, snapdragon-like flowers over moist Southern prairies and ditches, spreading into showy colonies for pollinators.
Finger obedient plant is a tall Southeastern obedient plant with stout spikes of pale pink-lavender flowers, a bold midsummer nectar source for moist, sunny gardens.
Beautiful false dragonhead is a Texas obedient plant with showy rose-purple flower spikes in spring, spreading in moist soils to feed early bees and hummingbirds.
Eastern false dragonhead is a wetland obedient plant of Southeastern bogs and marsh edges, with rose-pink flower spikes over a long season, excellent for rain gardens.

False indigo bush is a fast nitrogen-fixing shrub with spikes of tiny purple flowers lit by orange anthers, a larval host for several butterflies. Tolerates wet or dry; can spread vigorously, so site with care.
Cluster leadplant is a low Southeastern false indigo with frothy white-to-pale-purple flower spikes, a nitrogen-fixing, butterfly-hosting subshrub for dry, sandy ground.
Dwarf leadplant is a knee-high prairie false indigo with fragrant rose-purple flower spikes, a deep-rooted, nitrogen-fixing little shrub for dry prairies and rock gardens.
Fringed bluestar is a fine, threadleaf bluestar of dry Southeastern sandhills, with clusters of soft blue star-flowers in spring and golden, feathery foliage in fall. Deer-resistant.

Eastern bluestar is a dependable native perennial topped with clusters of powder-blue stars in spring, forming a shrubby mound that turns brilliant golden-yellow in fall. Deer-resistant and adaptable.
Light poppymallow opens pale pink-to-white cup flowers over a low mound on dry prairies and glades, a long-blooming, drought-proof native for sunny, lean soils.
Woodland poppymallow trails magenta, chalice-shaped 'winecup' flowers across the ground from a deep taproot, a heat- and drought-tolerant Southeastern native for sunny banks.
Glade lobelia is a late-blooming Southeastern wetland lobelia with rich blue flowers in fall, lighting up wet pine savannas and bog edges when little else blooms.
Blue wildrye is a fast cool-season bunchgrass with blue-green blades and upright seed spikes, widespread in western woodlands and meadows. A quick-establishing host and cover grass.
Thickspike wheatgrass is a sod-forming, rhizomatous cool-season grass of the western plains, prized for erosion control and revegetation on dry, disturbed ground.
Slender wheatgrass is a short-lived, widely adaptable cool-season bunchgrass used across the West for quick cover and restoration on a range of soils.
Western fescue is a fine-leaved, shade-tolerant bunchgrass of western forests and woodland edges, forming soft tufts under trees where few grasses thrive.
Red fescue is a fine-textured, rhizomatous cool-season grass used as a native turf and meadow grass, tolerating shade, salt, and a wide range of soils.
American dunegrass is a stout, blue-gray, rhizomatous grass that binds coastal and Great Lakes dunes, salt- and wind-tolerant and vital for shoreline stabilization.
Beardless wildrye is a vigorous, rhizomatous western grass that spreads into uniform blue-green stands, used for erosion control and restoration on alkaline and seasonally wet soils.
Alpine bluegrass is a low, tufted cool-season grass of high mountain meadows and tundra, cold-hardy and compact for rock gardens and high-elevation restoration.
Texas bluegrass is a rhizomatous, cool-season native bluegrass that stays green through mild winters, valued for cover and forage on the southern plains.
Sandberg bluegrass is a drought-tough, early-greening bunchgrass of the dry Intermountain West, an important early-season forage and a backbone of sagebrush-steppe restoration.

Purple giant hyssop is a tall, aromatic mint with soft lavender-purple flower spikes that hum with bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds from midsummer on. Deer-resistant and a superb nectar plant for moist sun to part shade.

Gopherweed is a bushy Southeastern wild indigo with bright yellow pea flowers over blue-green trifoliate leaves, a nitrogen-fixing, deer-resistant native for dry sandy soils and a host for sulphur butterflies.

Sensitive partridge pea is an annual legume with small yellow flowers and leaflets that fold when touched, a nitrogen-fixer whose extrafloral nectaries feed ants and pollinators and whose foliage hosts sulphur butterflies.

Red turtlehead bears rose-pink, turtle-head-shaped flowers in late summer over moist, sunny-to-shaded ground, a key larval host for the Baltimore checkerspot and a bumblebee favorite. Excellent for rain gardens.
Feather dalea is a fine-textured desert shrub with feathery rose-purple flowers, a nitrogen-fixing, deer-resistant pollinator plant exquisitely adapted to the hottest, driest Southwestern soils.

Showy tick trefoil is a tall prairie legume with bright rose-purple pea flowers loved by bumblebees, fixing nitrogen and hosting many skipper and hairstreak larvae. Its velcro-like seeds hitch rides on passersby.
Illinois tick trefoil is a deep-rooted, drought-tough prairie legume with pink pea flowers and jointed seed pods, a nitrogen-fixer and larval host for skippers and hairstreaks on dry ground.
Butterweed is a fast annual that floods wet bottomlands and ditches with bright golden flat-topped flowers in spring, an early nectar source that self-sows freely on moist, sunny ground.
Prairie groundsel lifts cheerful clusters of yellow daisies over basal rosettes on dry prairies and glades in spring, a tough, deer-resistant early-season pollinator plant.

Cliff stonecrop is a low, evergreen native succulent that mats over rock outcrops and walls with blue-gray rosettes and starry white flowers, a drought- and deer-resistant groundcover for sun to part shade.
Mountain goldenbanner sends up lupine-like spikes of bright yellow pea flowers in western mountain meadows, a nitrogen-fixing, deer-resistant native that spreads into golden drifts.
Indian's dream is a small, wiry evergreen rock fern of dry western slopes and serpentine outcrops, tolerating sun and drought where most ferns can't.
Black-stem spleenwort is a dainty evergreen fern that tucks into limestone crevices and walls, with dark wiry stems and ladder-like fronds.
Maidenhair spleenwort is a delicate little evergreen fern of shaded rock crevices, forming tidy rosettes of beaded fronds on cliffs and old walls.
Rattlesnake fern is a woodland grape fern with a single triangular, lacy frond and an upright spore stalk, a quiet curiosity of rich, moist shade.
Lace lip fern is a drought-hardy evergreen rock fern of the Pacific Northwest, its finely cut fronds curling tight in dry spells and reviving with rain.
Bulblet fern is a graceful, long-tapering fern of moist shaded limestone, dropping tiny bulblets along its fronds that root into new colonies.
Fragile fern is a dainty early-season fern of shaded rocks and ledges, with brittle stems and lacy fronds that emerge with the spring thaw.
Spinulose wood fern is an adaptable, lacy-fronded wood fern of moist forests and swamp edges, spreading gently into soft colonies in shade.
Crested wood fern is a wetland fern of swamps and marsh edges, with distinctive ladder-like fertile fronds held stiffly upright. Tolerates standing water.
Southern wood fern is a bold, glossy evergreen fern of Southeastern wet woods and seeps, with tall arching fronds that stay green through mild winters.
Scouring rush is a striking, leafless evergreen horsetail of jointed green stems, prized for architectural form in wet ground and containers. Spreads aggressively by rhizome — contain it.
Oak fern is a delicate, ground-hugging fern of cool northern forests, spreading by thin rhizomes into airy triangular-fronded carpets in deep shade.
Sword fern is the native Florida fern behind the familiar Boston fern, with arching evergreen fronds that spread by runners into lush groundcover in warm, moist shade.
Royal fern is a magnificent large fern with flower-like fertile tips, thriving at pond edges, in bogs, and along streams in sun to part shade with constant moisture.
Purple cliffbrake is a drought-tough evergreen fern of dry limestone cliffs and ledges, with blue-gray fronds on dark purple stems. A rock-garden gem for lean, alkaline sites.
California polypody is a small evergreen fern that greens up with winter rains on shaded rocks and banks, then rests through the dry summer. A natural for California shade gardens.
Leathery polypody is a thick-fronded evergreen fern of the foggy Pacific coast, growing on rocks and even tree trunks, tolerant of wind and salt spray.
Narrowleaf sword fern is a compact, drought-tolerant evergreen sword fern of dry rocky western slopes, holding stiff upright fronds through summer dryness.
Bracken is the widespread native fern of open woods, barrens, and clearings, with large triangular fronds. Vigorous and rhizomatous — best for naturalizing wild areas, not tidy beds.
Marsh fern is a fine-textured fern of sunny marshes, wet meadows, and pond edges, spreading by rhizome to form soft stands in saturated ground.
Massachusetts fern is a delicate fern of acidic Northeastern swamps and boggy woods, spreading slowly into airy colonies in cool, wet shade.
Blunt-lobed woodsia is a tough little fern of rocky woods, ledges, and old walls, forming tidy clumps that tolerate more sun and dryness than most ferns.
Giant chain fern is the largest western native fern, raising towering evergreen fronds from seeps, streamsides, and wet shaded slopes. A dramatic specimen for moist gardens.
Virginia chain fern spreads by rhizome through acidic bogs, marshes, and wet woods of the East, its bold fronds tinged red in spring. A robust native for sunny to shaded wetlands.
Spike bentgrass is an adaptable western bentgrass of moist meadows and streambanks, used in restoration and erosion control on a range of wet-to-mesic soils.
Rough bentgrass (ticklegrass) is a delicate, widespread cool-season grass whose diffuse, hair-fine seed heads catch the light and break off to tumble in the wind.
Blue maidencane is a Florida wetland grass of wet flatwoods and marsh edges, spreading by rhizome to provide cover and seed in saturated, sandy ground.
Wiregrass is the keystone bunchgrass of the longleaf pine savanna, carrying ground fire that the ecosystem depends on. Essential for Southeastern sandhill and savanna restoration.
Arrowfeather threeawn is a slender native bunchgrass of dry sandy barrens and open woods, with feathery purplish seed heads. A larval host and cover grass for lean soils.
California brome is a fast cool-season bunchgrass of western woodlands and grasslands, widely used for quick cover and erosion control after fire or disturbance.
Prairie brome is a graceful native brome of prairies and open woods, with nodding, drooping seed heads, a well-behaved cool-season grass for meadow plantings.
Fragrant flatsedge is a robust annual-to-short-lived sedge of wet shores, ditches, and pond edges, its golden-brown seed heads feeding waterfowl and songbirds.
Saltgrass is a tough, rhizomatous grass of salt marshes, alkaline flats, and coastal dunes, one of the few plants that thrives in saline soil. Key for salt-marsh restoration.
American mannagrass is a tall wetland grass of marshes, ditches, and pond margins, with airy seed panicles; its seeds and foliage are important waterfowl and wildlife food.
California sweetgrass is a fragrant, vanilla-scented woodland grass of the Pacific states, spreading gently in moist shade. Culturally significant and pleasantly aromatic.
Curly mesquite is a low, stoloniferous Southwestern grass that knits dry desert grasslands into tough sod, valuable forage and groundcover for arid, alkaline soils.
Black needlerush is the sharp-tipped, blue-gray rush that dominates vast Southeastern salt and brackish marshes. A salt-tolerant backbone of tidal-marsh habitat and restoration.
Rice cutgrass is a wetland grass of marsh and pond edges with rough, blade-edged leaves and seeds favored by waterfowl. Spreads in saturated ground; site away from paths.
Green sprangletop is a drought-tough warm-season bunchgrass of Southwestern grasslands and rocky slopes, with open, sprangly seed heads. Good forage and cover for arid sites.
Coast Range melic is a delicate California bunchgrass of dry, shaded slopes and chaparral edges, with airy one-sided seed sprays. A graceful native for dry shade.
Three-flower melic is a cool-season woodland bunchgrass of rocky bluffs and open woods, with nodding seed clusters, well suited to dry shade and naturalized plantings.
Gulfdune paspalum is a rhizomatous warm-season grass of Southeastern coastal flatwoods and dune swales, salt-tolerant cover for wet, sandy ground.
Alpine timothy is a compact cool-season bunchgrass of high mountain meadows, with dense cylindrical seed heads, hardy and tidy for cold, moist, high-elevation sites.
Tobosa grass is a rugged, rhizomatous Southwestern grass of clay flats and swales, drought-proof forage and cover that greens up fast after summer rains.
Bluebunch wheatgrass is the signature bunchgrass of the sagebrush steppe and a workhorse of western restoration, with blue-green tufts and slender seed spikes. Drought-hardy and long-lived.
Starrush whitetop is an ornamental sedge whose flowers are ringed by showy, star-like white bracts, lighting up wet pinelands and rain gardens. A standout for bog plantings.
Giant whitetop is a larger relative of starrush whitetop, with bold white star-bracts over wet Southeastern savannas and ditches. Striking and easy in moist, sunny ground.
Woolgrass is a bulrush-like sedge topped with woolly, tawny seed heads in late summer, a bold native for marshes, pond edges, and rain gardens. Cover and seed for wetland wildlife.
Smooth cordgrass is the foundational grass of Atlantic and Gulf salt marshes, building and binding tidal mud between the tides. Essential for coastal marsh restoration.
Saltmeadow cordgrass forms swirling 'cowlick' meadows in the high salt marsh and on back dunes, a fine-textured, salt- and drought-tolerant grass for coastal plantings.
Gulf cordgrass is a stout, fountain-like bunchgrass of coastal prairies and marsh edges, tolerant of salt, drought, and heavy soils. A bold accent and tough cover grass.
Spike trisetum is a compact cool-season bunchgrass of alpine tundra and rocky high country worldwide, with dense bristly seed spikes, hardy for cold rock gardens.
Red baneberry brightens rich woods with fluffy white flower clusters, followed by glossy red berries on the foliage. Striking but note: all parts, especially the berries, are toxic.
White colicroot sends up slender wands of mealy-textured white tubular flowers from a flat basal rosette, a distinctive native of moist sandy meadows and open pine woods.
Yellow colicroot is the Southeastern counterpart of white colicroot, with golden-yellow tubular flowers on tall stalks above a rosette, native to wet pine savannas.
Wild sarsaparilla carpets woodland floors with a single long-stalked compound leaf shading hidden greenish flower balls, then dark berries for wildlife. A fine woodland groundcover.
American spikenard is a bold, shrub-like woodland perennial with large divided leaves, airy sprays of tiny flowers, and dark purple berries relished by birds. A dramatic native for rich shade.
Devil's walking stick is a fiercely spiny large shrub or small tree topped in summer with huge clouds of white flowers, then purple-black berries for birds. Bold, suckering, and architectural.

Jack-in-the-pulpit is the iconic woodland aroid, its hooded green-and-purple spathe sheltering a 'jack' spadix, ripening to a cluster of brilliant red berries. Foliage contains irritating calcium oxalate.
American bellflower is a tall, airy native (annual or biennial in habit) with flat, star-shaped blue flowers up leafy spires, blooming for weeks in moist woodland edges and clearings.
Fairywand sends up a slender, nodding white flower spike (showier on male plants) over a basal rosette in rich woods, a charming and distinctive native of moist, shaded slopes.
Carolina spring beauty is a woodland spring ephemeral with pink-veined white flowers over a pair of broad leaves, carpeting rich forest floors before fading by early summer.
Western spring beauty is a montane ephemeral that emerges at snowmelt with pink-striped white flowers, its edible corms ('mountain potatoes') long gathered by Indigenous peoples.
Siberian miner's lettuce (candyflower) is a Pacific coast woodland annual/perennial with pink-and-white flowers and edible, succulent foliage, self-sowing in cool, moist shade.
Bluebead lily carries nodding greenish-yellow bells over glossy basal leaves in cool northern woods, followed by striking porcelain-blue berries. A choice native for acidic, shaded gardens.
Queen's cup is a dainty western woodland lily with a single upturned white flower over a few basal leaves, ripening to a metallic-blue berry. A gem for cool, moist conifer shade.
Goldthread is a tiny evergreen groundcover of cool bogs and conifer woods, with glossy three-parted leaves, small white starry flowers, and bright gold underground runners.
Virginia waterleaf spreads into leafy colonies in rich moist woods, topped with clusters of bell-shaped white-to-lavender flowers whose long stamens give a frothy look. Bees love it.
Sweet cicely is a woodland member of the carrot family with ferny leaves and airy white flower umbels; its roots and seeds smell and taste of anise. An easy native filler for shade.
Hooker's fairybells is a western woodland perennial with arching stems, hidden creamy bells beneath the leaves, and orange-red berries. A graceful native for moist conifer shade.
Smith's fairybells is a Pacific coast woodland lily with large, tubular creamy-white bells dangling beneath bright green leaves, followed by orange berries. Lovely in cool, moist shade.
Canadian burnet raises bottlebrush spikes of fuzzy white flowers over ferny foliage in wet meadows and bogs late in the season, a graceful, pollinator-rich native for sunny damp ground.
California figwort bears small but nectar-rich maroon-green flowers on tall stems that are a magnet for bees, wasps, and hummingbirds. Unshowy to us, irresistible to pollinators.
Starflower is a dainty northern woodlander with a whorl of leaves topped by delicate white star-shaped flowers on thread-fine stalks, spreading into colonies in cool acidic shade.
California false hellebore is a bold western wet-meadow plant with broad pleated leaves and tall plumes of starry white-green flowers. Architectural but highly toxic — handle and site with care.
Virginia bunchflower lifts airy, branching panicles of starry white flowers above grass-like leaves in wet Southeastern meadows and savannas. Striking in bloom and, like its kin, toxic.
Green false hellebore unfurls dramatic pleated leaves in spring wet meadows and swamps, followed by branched spikes of green star-flowers. A bold structural native — all parts are very toxic.

Yellowroot is a low, suckering woodland shrub forming a fine-textured groundcover, with drooping strings of tiny star flowers in spring and yellow fall color. Excellent for shaded slopes and streambanks.
Fremont's star lily opens early clusters of creamy star-shaped flowers from a bulb on California's grassy and rocky slopes. Handsome in spring but, as a death camas, toxic to people and livestock.
Sweetflag is an iris-like marginal aquatic with aromatic sword leaves and a finger-like greenish flower spike, spreading along pond and stream edges in shallow water.
Yerba mansa is a Southwestern wetland groundcover with cone-shaped flower heads ringed by white bracts, spreading by runners over wet, even alkaline, ground. Aromatic and traditionally medicinal.
False nettle is a stingless nettle relative of wet woods and marsh edges, an essential larval host for red admiral, question mark, and comma butterflies. Unshowy but ecologically valuable.
White marsh marigold opens glistening white, golden-centered flowers at the edges of snowmelt streams and wet alpine meadows in the western mountains.
Marsh marigold lights up early-spring wetlands with waxy golden flowers over glossy round leaves, a cheerful native for pond edges, ditches, and rain gardens. Foliage is toxic raw.
Golden canna is the native canna of Southeastern marshes and ditches, with bold paddle leaves and large soft-yellow flowers loved by hummingbirds. A tropical-looking accent for wet, sunny ground.
Evening rain lily bursts into fragrant white, moth-pollinated flowers within days of summer rains, then waits underground for the next storm. A magical, deer-resistant bulb for the South.
Hill Country rain lily sends up crisp white flowers after spring rains across Texas prairies and roadsides, a tough, deer-resistant bulb that naturalizes in sunny, well-drained ground.
Copper rain lily flushes coppery-yellow, wind-flared flowers after late-summer storms, a diminutive, free-naturalizing bulb for hot, sunny Southern gardens.
Mangrove spider lily is a robust coastal Florida bulb with extravagant, fragrant white spider-flowers and strappy leaves, thriving in wet, sandy, salt-touched ground.
Spring spider lily lifts large, fragrant white spider-flowers over wet ditches, marsh edges, and prairies of the Gulf states each spring. A striking, deer-resistant native bulb.
Alligator lily is a slender Florida spider lily of wet pine flatwoods and marshes, with delicate fragrant white flowers, perfect for sunny bog and rain gardens.
Seashore mallow is a salt-tolerant brackish-marsh mallow with weeks of soft-pink hibiscus-like flowers, excellent for sunny pond edges, rain gardens, and coastal plantings.
Sea lavender hovers a haze of tiny lavender flowers over salt-marsh rosettes, prized fresh or dried in arrangements. A salt-loving native for coastal and brackish gardens.
Water cowbane is a slender Southeastern wetland member of the carrot family, with flat white flower umbels feeding pollinators in wet pine savannas and marsh edges.

Frogfruit is a tough, mat-forming groundcover dotted year-round with tiny white-pink flowers, a tireless nectar source and larval host for phaon crescent and buckeye butterflies. A superb lawn alternative.
Swamp smartweed lines marsh and pond edges with slender spikes of pink-white flowers; its seeds are a key food for waterfowl and marsh birds. Spreads in saturated ground.
Dotted smartweed is a wetland smartweed with gland-dotted leaves and greenish-white flower spikes, a wildlife food plant for the margins of ponds, ditches, and swamps.
Juniper leaf is a low, wiry mat-forming native with needle-like leaves and tiny white flowers, colonizing open sandy and disturbed ground as a tough little groundcover.
Mock bishopweed is a delicate, ferny-leaved annual of the carrot family that froths with lacy white flower umbels in wet meadows and ditches, a magnet for tiny pollinators.
Savanna meadow beauty is a tall, blue-leaved meadow beauty with luminous rose-pink, buzz-pollinated flowers in wet pine savannas, a graceful native for sunny bog gardens.
Maryland meadow beauty spreads into colonies of pale-pink, golden-anthered flowers in damp meadows, ditches, and savannas, blooming for months in sunny, moist ground.
Fringed meadow beauty is a compact Southeastern meadow beauty with rose-purple flowers and fringed anthers, a charming little native for sunny, wet, acidic sites.
Texas star is an annual rose-gentian with starry pink, yellow-eyed flowers brightening prairies and fields in early summer, an easy self-sowing native for sunny meadows.
Large marsh pink crowns coastal-plain marshes and wet savannas with big, glowing rose-pink, yellow-centered flowers, one of the showiest natives for sunny wet ground.

White-topped pitcher plant is a spectacular carnivorous bog plant with tall, white-and-crimson-veined pitchers that trap insects, plus nodding red spring flowers. Needs constant moisture, sun, and acidic, nutrient-poor soil.
Marsh St. John's wort is a wetland relative of the St. John's worts, with soft-pink flowers and reddish foliage in acidic bogs, marshes, and pond edges.
California pipevine is the sole larval host of the California pipevine swallowtail, with curious U-shaped purple-brown 'pipe' flowers in late winter. A must for butterfly gardeners in the West.
Chaparral false bindweed is a well-behaved native morning-glory relative of California slopes, with cream-white funnel flowers, far tamer than the weedy bindweeds.
Pine hyacinth is an unusual erect, non-climbing clematis of Florida pinelands, with solitary nodding lavender bells over fine foliage, blooming much of the year.
Rock clematis is a delicate western mountain vine that drapes shrubs and rocks with nodding blue-violet bell flowers in late spring, followed by silky seed plumes.
Pipestem clematis (chaparral clematis) scrambles over California chaparral with creamy starry flowers in spring and showy feathery seed heads after. Drought-tolerant once established.
Western virgin's bower is a vigorous western vine that smothers fences and streamside thickets in masses of white flowers, then silvery seed plumes. Fast and drought-hardy — give it room.
Western blue clematis is a slender northern and montane vine with nodding violet-blue lantern flowers in spring and feathery seed heads, charming on a trellis or scrambling over shrubs.
Pitcher's leatherflower is a refined native vine hung with thick-petaled, urn-shaped purple bells through summer, a well-mannered climber for trellises and fences.
Scarlet leatherflower is a Texas endemic vine with nodding, urn-shaped true-red bells that hummingbirds visit, blooming over a long season on a tidy, drought-tolerant climber.
Carolina snailseed is a twining native vine grown for its glossy, brilliant-red berry clusters in fall that birds devour, with inconspicuous greenish flowers. A good native screen.
Devil's potato is an evergreen twining vine of South Florida hammocks and pinelands, with fragrant white pinwheel flowers, a larval host for the faithful beauty moth.
Swamp jessamine is a twining Southeastern vine with fragrant yellow trumpet flowers in spring and again in fall, thriving in wet, sunny-to-shaded ground. All parts are toxic.
Lindheimer's globeberry is a drought-tough Texas cucurbit vine that sprouts each year from a huge caudex, with small flowers and shiny red berries for birds. A curiosity for xeric gardens.
Moonflower unfurls huge, fragrant white trumpets at dusk for night-flying moths, a fast tropical-looking native vine for warm, sunny trellises and arbors.
Scarlet morning glory is a fast annual vine hung with small brilliant-red trumpets that hummingbirds chase from late summer into fall. Self-sows in warm, sunny gardens.
Bush morning glory is a non-climbing, mounding perennial morning glory of the Great Plains, with rose-purple funnels over a massive deep root. Exceptionally drought-proof for hot, sandy sites.
Railroad vine is a fast, salt- and drought-proof beach groundcover that races across dunes on long runners, dotted with pink-purple morning-glory flowers. A premier coastal dune stabilizer.
Anglepod milkvine is a twining milkweed relative of woodland edges, with star-shaped greenish-purple flowers and warty pods; like its kin it can host monarch and queen larvae.
Climbing hempvine is a vigorous native vine that drapes wet thickets and marsh edges with clusters of fuzzy white flowers in fall, a major late-season nectar source. Give it room to ramble.
Fetid passionflower is a delicate annual-to-tender passionvine with lacy-bracted white-and-pink flowers and small edible fruit, a larval host for gulf fritillary and other longwings.
Yellow passionflower is a dainty native passionvine with small greenish-yellow flowers and purple-black berries, a shade-tolerant larval host for gulf fritillary and zebra longwing.
Birdwing passionflower is a fine-leaved desert passionvine of Texas and the Southwest, with tiny green-yellow flowers, a drought-hardy larval host for fritillaries and longwings.
Doctorbush (wild plumbago) is a sprawling, shrubby vine of the South with white phlox-like flowers nearly year-round, a larval host for the cassius blue butterfly and tolerant of dry shade.

Lechuguilla is the signature agave of the Chihuahuan Desert, forming spiny blue-green rosettes that send up a tall yellow flower spike once before dying. Tough architectural form for hot, rocky gardens.
Buckhorn cholla is a branching, spiny desert cholla with bright yellow-to-red cup flowers in spring, providing nesting cover for desert birds. A sculptural, drought-proof cactus.
Desert Christmas cactus is a slender, pencil-stemmed cholla studded with bright red berries through winter that birds and mammals relish. A wiry, drought-hardy desert accent.
Green sotol forms a fountain of slender, saw-toothed green leaves topped in summer by a towering creamy flower stalk. A bold, drought-proof desert accent.
Texas sotol is a rounded rosette of fine, toothed leaves that throws up a tall plume of tiny cream flowers, an elegant, heat- and drought-tolerant evergreen for xeric landscapes.
Claret cup cactus forms mounds of spiny stems crowned with brilliant scarlet cup flowers that hummingbirds visit, one of the showiest and hardiest native cacti.
Texas rainbow cactus is a solitary hedgehog cactus banded in colored spines, bearing large, glossy yellow-to-pink flowers in spring. A jewel for hot, well-drained gardens.
Pitaya is a sprawling, clumping hedgehog cactus with magenta flowers and sweet, edible strawberry-flavored fruit, drought-proof for rocky desert gardens.
Strawberry hedgehog forms big mounds of straw-colored spines topped with magenta flowers and edible, strawberry-like fruit, a showy, heat-loving native cactus.
Nylon hedgehog is a small, cold-hardy cactus of the high plains with unusual lemon-scented green-yellow flowers, tough enough for rock gardens far north of the desert.
Chaparral yucca builds a perfect sphere of rigid blue-gray blades, then sends up a spectacular towering spike of creamy bells once before dying. Pollinated solely by the yucca moth.
False aloe is a soft-leaved, agave relative forming a flat rosette of fleshy spotted leaves and a tall spike of fragrant greenish-yellow night-flowers. A curious, drought-hardy native for sun.
Foothill beargrass forms a grassy fountain of long arching leaves with plumes of creamy flowers, a tough, fine-textured evergreen for dry, rocky Southwestern slopes.
Parry's beargrass is a large, dramatic beargrass with a dense rosette of stiff leaves and a massive creamy flower stalk, an architectural focal point for desert gardens.
Texas sacahuista is a low, grass-like beargrass forming dense mounds of fine evergreen leaves with flower clusters nestled among them, drought-proof groundcover for hot, dry sites.
Eastern prickly pear is the cold-hardy native prickly pear of the East, a low mat of flat pads with silky yellow flowers and edible red fruit. Surprisingly winter-hardy for a cactus.
Purple prickly pear is prized for its violet-tinged pads (deepest in cold and drought) and yellow, red-centered flowers, a striking, drought-proof cactus for hot gardens.
Plains prickly pear is a low, cold-hardy prickly pear of the Great Plains with yellow (sometimes red-centered) flowers and a deep storage root, tough for dry, sunny meadows.
Tulip prickly pear is a widespread, variable prickly pear with large yellow flowers and purple-red fruit (tunas), a drought-proof, wildlife-rich cactus for Western xeric landscapes.
Organ pipe cactus is a magnificent multi-stemmed columnar cactus of the Sonoran Desert, with night-blooming flowers (pollinated by bats and moths) and sweet edible fruit. A bold desert specimen.
Buckley's yucca forms a trunkless rosette of narrow, curly-fibered blue-green leaves topped by a tall spike of white bells, a tidy, cold- and drought-hardy yucca for Texas gardens.
Twistleaf yucca is a Texas Hill Country endemic with distinctively twisting, red-edged green leaves and a tall white flower spike, well suited to rocky limestone shade or sun.
Torrey yucca is a bold trunk-forming yucca of the Chihuahuan Desert, with stiff bayonet leaves and large clusters of waxy white flowers. A statuesque specimen for xeric landscapes.
Spanish dagger is a stout, trunked yucca with sharp blue-green blades and huge spring clusters of creamy bells, a dramatic, drought- and cold-tolerant evergreen for the South.
Redshanks is a tall, airy chaparral shrub with shredding red bark and feathery foliage, topped in summer with frothy white flowers. A drought-proof, fire-adapted evergreen for hot, dry slopes.
Silver sagebrush is a silvery, aromatic sagebrush of moist western flats and meadow edges, providing essential cover and forage for sage-steppe wildlife. Cold- and drought-hardy.
Sand sagebrush is a fine, thread-leaved aromatic shrub that stabilizes deep sands across the southern plains, with a soft silvery texture and high wildlife value.

White sagebrush (western mugwort) is a silvery, aromatic rhizomatous perennial that spreads into drifts of ghostly foliage, a larval host for the American lady butterfly and a deer-resistant filler. Can run, so give it room.
Black sagebrush is a low, dense, aromatic sagebrush of dry rocky western ranges, key winter forage and cover in the sagebrush steppe. Compact and very drought-tolerant.
Sandhill sage is a soft, silver-white coastal California subshrub forming low aromatic mounds on dunes and bluffs, salt- and drought-tolerant for seaside gardens.
Groundsel tree (sea myrtle) is a tough coastal shrub that erupts in showy white seed-plumes in fall, a major late-season nectar source for migrating pollinators. Salt- and flood-tolerant.
Fernbush is an aromatic, drought-proof western shrub with ferny foliage and spires of white spirea-like flowers in summer, excellent for hot, dry, well-drained gardens.
Bush chinquapin is a low evergreen chaparral shrub of western mountains with golden-backed leaves and spiny burs holding sweet edible nuts that feed wildlife. Drought- and fire-hardy.
